1.2 Relation to the BBI-JU Annual Work Plan  
Our BIOnTOP proposal, addressing the topic BBI.2018. SO3.R10 - Develop bio-based packaging products that are biodegradable/compostable and/or recyclable, will contribute significantly to the KPIs set out in the Strategic Innovation and Research Agenda (SIRA)5 and is fully aligned with the BBI roadmap as we will further discuss in section 2. Our overriding goal aligns with its 4 strategic orientations (SO) (fig. 2): SO1) we will use biomass derived feedstocks (significantly >85%, although minor non bio-based compounds, e.g. processing aids or catalysts, may be used if no alternative exists); favouring local resources of second or third generation wherever possible, e.g. waste from agro-industrial value chains: plasticizers, malic acid as well as lactic acid from fruit processing, FA from oil by-products, and wheat fillers as milling residue; SO2) the core of our activities will deal with the eco-design of packaging based on novel copolymers and compounds from selected bio-based building blocks; SO3) we will tackle the requirements of several mass products together worth 192 bn €/year for which the materials will be validated (SO4) awareness raising campaigns will allow us to meet the needs of our EU end user industries as well as the consumers’ demand for greener products. Besides creating socio-economic value, our project will also help the EU in implementing better waste management strategies as well as reducing the major societal challenge reflected in the European Plastics strategy. Further extending the impact of the BBI-JU, we also have beneficiaries from countries where no BBI participant in awarded funded projects are based like Czech Republic, as well as for personal care & pharma packaging which are, to our knowledge, not yet covered in the running projects. We further cover cross cutting advanced materials and processing KETs. The detailed relation of our proposal to the specific challenge and scope addressed by the topic are outlined in table 1 below:  
Table 1: How BIOnTOP relates to the scope and challenges of the BBI 2018.SO3.R10 topic  
Topic text  How BIOnTOP addresses these challenges/scope  
To make the packaging EoL significantly more sustainable.  
Design new processing systems for functional bio-based packaging products that are reusable, recyclable, and/or compostable and biodegradable in various environments to reduce their overall environmental footprint, as an alternative to the current identified benchmark products.  
The packaging formats that we focus on are generally both neither renewably sourced, nor recyclable at EoL independently of being based on monomaterials (light packaging) or multimaterials (multilayer or coated products). As seen, even biodegradable polymers have weaknesses in terms of use (properties) or even EoL (limited biodegradability in mild conditions or at high thickness). Innovations in the area of bio-based packaging give unique opportunities to make their production and use more circular. As we will test in WP4, our new bio-packaging will be eco-designed to be compatible with the most suitable end of life options on a case to case basis according to best practice studies from ISWA (member of our AB) on a general and regional basis. Applying Circular Economy (CE) principle, reusable packaging (bags & wraps) will be delivered to maintain the resources in use at their outmost initial value for longer. For single use materials and at the end of the multiple use packaging where applicable, final material recycling will be considered. Since the upstream sorting of EoL waste is not always optimal, we will check that each material can be properly identified as a pre-requisite to be handled in the right waste management stream and, if applicable, recycled into new materials for packaging or textile applications (we can note that there are increasing efforts for recycling PLA as already done by ROMEI). For applications contaminated with organic substances after use, different composting routes will be available. Our solutions will be suitable for industrial composting even at high thickness where this EoL option is available, for home composting and for AD. Soil and marine biodegradability will also be tested. Where applicable, eco-toxicity tests will be performed on the developed materials along with the biodegradation trials.  
The eco-design of our new materials will be further enabled by the novel biodegradation predictive models developed in WP4. All these materials and processes along the product life cycle will allow us to meet society’s and users’ demands to improve the EoL options to avoid landfilling and support manufacturers, who are responsible for the suitability of the materials they commercialise, to enforce relevant waste legislations.  
Incrementing our circularity, we will use biomass by-products as raw materials fibres from wheat bran, monomers such as lactic and malic acids from fruit waste, coatings from cheese by-products and nano-scale surface modifications from ubiquitous fatty acids from plant oils (most sustainable will be selected). Although outside of our scope that is rather on later packaging conversion, research into biorefining additional building blocks such as LA from second (non-edible) and third generation (waste) feedstock at suitable purity is currently pursued intensely. As soon as on the market, those could be substituted by the relevant monomers to further increase our BIOnTOP sustainability.  
In case of multilayer materials, we will either resort to removable coatings (whey protein +FA grafting), so separated monomaterial can be obtained in the pre-washing step of the recycling process, or to coated textiles using similar polymeric matrix as the textile it is applied on.  
In case of multi-layer products, consider the feasibility of producing multi-layer/single-polymer solutions, provided they meet all required functionalities and outperform state-of-the-art alternatives for sustainability.  
Other innovations in functionality and production are assets.  
In terms of multifunctionality brought by our coatings, we will bring innovations in terms of providing packaging films and textiles with: i) easy emptying behaviour (relevant to minimise residual product at EoL of e.g. cosmetic sachets), ii) non-stick properties (relevant to improve consumer experience e.g. for cheese release lids), iii) waterproof properties (e.g. for carrier/shopping bags). Furthermore, our protein barrier coatings also ensure UV barrier while keeping coated film transparency, again a preferable feature vs. e.g. non-transparent metallised barrier. Finally, alginates will be used as aroma barrier for tea bags providing an added asset vs. fossil counterparts or emerging PLA solutions.  
Further maximising resource efficiency (optimising properties and use of materials by barrier layers that can reduce packaging thickness), the materials to be delivered will have a number of unique selling points vs. conventional counterparts: i) close to 100% bio-sourced, ii) superior food preservation in case of barrier packaging, iii) meeting converters’ needs in terms of processability (standard converting techniques used also for novel materials) and iv) EoL options adjustable to the respective applications. Other properties like mechanical, thermal resistance, optical and barrier properties, etc., will be tuned to match the requirements of each of the selected applications employing or combining several approaches, e.g. modular copolymers structure, fillers, REX, etc. (see section 1.4). Benchmarking will be done vs. currently broadly used fossil and emerging bioplastic solutions along with the validation of the new materials for each application and economic as well as environmental sustainability studies of both delivered products and processes to demonstrate such comparison on a cradle to cradle basis.  
Address the production process, including the necessary improvements to coating and chemical FA grafting steps to obtain the target end-products and their specifications.  
TRL3 to 5.  
We will develop different approaches for obtaining biopolymer-based packaging for multiple food and personal care products as well as multisector bags (secondary packaging) through innovative processes. We will also evaluate the theoretical applicability of our materials to other applications (further textiles products, pharma packaging, among others). Therefore, we will initially select different monomers, fillers and additives that will be compounded with PLA, as available bio-based polymer, but also with new BIOnTOP copolymers. We will upscale the coating process for both plastic films and textiles. This encompasses new coatings processes based on whey protein and their further top coating using fully bio-based fatty acid anhydrides nanolayer. If needed, in specific packaging formats, lamination will be performed but it is not expected to be modified while using our new materials.  
The start, progress and end TRL of each technical component is justified in table 3. In each WP, from the bioplastics & coatings development in WP2-3, to the production and characterisation of the derived bio-based packaging for each target application (WP5-6), we will work subsequently at lab and pilot scale obtaining significant amounts of material for an industrially relevant further characterisation and validation of the demonstrators. Different techno-economical aspects of scalability will be considered in WP5-6 to give recommendations for successful transfer and sustainable production scale up, so the results TRL is further matured to reach the market.  
Actively industry The whole circular bio-based value chain is covered by our BIOnTOP partners. All WPs involve industrial partners in participation to demonstrate integration potential for the developed concepts into current industrial landscapes or existing plants towards quicker deployment and scaled up to apply industrial-wide.  
The supply of the raw materials as well as in the upscaling and testing of the new packaging materials based on the initial industrial requirements defined in WP1. Our consortium has a strong industry component including the participation of 9 SMEs, 6 Large enterprises and many more associated via membership in the participating industry association. 1.2 Relation to the Work Programme     
We address the topic LC-SPIRE-08-2020: “Novel high performance materials and components”; from a wider point of view, we perfectly fit the context where the topic is placed, i.e. the Leadership in enabling and industrial technologies – Nanotechnologies, Advanced Materials, Biotechnology, and Advanced Manufacturing and Processing Work Programme under the Sustainable Process Industry (SPIRE) section.  
We focus on the steel industry, one of the high-energy demanding sectors which are considered a key part of Europe’s manufacturing base and which represents, together with other sectors such as cement and chemicals, 20% of European industry in terms of both employment and turnover. The steel sector is characterised by energy intensive technologies and thus, among other targets, we have to optimise our industrial processes to reduce energy consumption and increase the use of renewable sources to make it environmentally sustainable.

1.2 Relation to the NMBP work plan  
We will design our DECOAT products to enable separation (and thus recycling) by incorporating smart additives in our adhesive that are activated by a specific trigger (e.g. heat, humidity, microwave, chemical, mechanical). Additionally, we will develop an efficient sorting process correlated with the trigger and a pilot recycling plant to illustrate circular use. In this fashion, our project will directly respond to two of the three types of materials design objectives put forth in the call scope: ‘Design of polymer material structures with intrinsic sorting/recycling abilities’ (bullet point 1) and ‘developments of separation and recycling technologies’ (bullet point 3).  
NMBP-26-2018 call - challenge & scope Our DECOAT response  
products with smart intrinsic recycling and/or sorting abilities  
We are targeting three product families using multilayer coated parts: backpacks, household electronics, and automotive parts. Each of them will be made from multi-layered materials that allow debonding for recycling and that can be sorted via the trigger mechanism. These are representative of a wide range of coated and painted textiles and plastic-based consumer goods.  
harmonise with circular economy principles  
The 3 basic circular economy principles are3: (i) Design out waste and pollution; (ii) Keep products and materials in use; (iii) Regenerate natural systems. We will enable coated, hybrid, and painted materials to harmonise with principles (i) and (ii).  
help industry to better match the EU environmental targets at the same time as improving their competitiveness  
We will target the re-use and recycling of waste materials from households to increase to a minimum of 50%4. We will mainly contribute for ‘plastics’. Our project will also contribute to the EU environmental targets for the automotive sector5.  
design of polymer formulations with smart additives  
We will make use of a toolbox of smart additives: microwaveable additives, microcapsules, or bonding/debonding adhesives for separation.  
developments of separation and recycling technologies  
We will tailor microwave technology, heat, steam, and dissolving to enable separation of the coating from the textile or plastic.  
demonstrate the actual circular use of such materials through re-processing of recycled products  
We will develop a pilot for the use cases to demonstrate the circular use of the materials. Both closed-loop (for same application) and open-loop (across applications but of the same value level) recycling will be considered.  
evaluation of properties of re-processed products in an industrial environment  
We will evaluate the properties of the use case products made with the reprocessed materials by the RTDs and by us (DECOAT end-users) according to our normal testing conditions.  
full Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) of the material production and life-cycle  
LCA is included in our DECOAT activities and is a Key Objective. Scope: raw material acquisition and processing, manufacturing, distribution and transport, use phase, end of life, and benefits and loads beyond system boundaries (secondary systems).  
Activities should start at TRL3, …achieve TRL5  
Our DECOAT approach fulfils this criterion, see section ‘positioning of DECOAT’

1.2 Relation to the DT-FOF-05 work plan  
Fit to the ‘Specific Challenge’ of the call (Topic identifier: DT-FOF-05-2019)  
We aim to stimulate the transfer from large-scale manufacturing to industrial companies of the Do It Yourself (DIY), fablabs, micro-factories, and makers approaches in the FMCG sector for powdered and liquid products. Our approach will lead to new machines (fablabs) and digital services (OI B2B/B2C digital platform services) that will facilitate value chain collaborations and consumer involvement in the design and rapid manufacture of customer-centric personalised and customized powdered/liquid FMCG. Our approach will reduce time to market for new personalised/customised FMCG, creating new business opportunities especially for SMEs and new actors in the powdered/liquid FMCG innovation ecosystem. Demonstrating our OI digital platform and fablabs with the relevant FMCG supply chain and consumers will raise awareness and confidence in the approach, allowing us to widely adopt these innovative approaches to engage consumers and respond to societal needs, taking individual preferences of women and men into account.  
Fit to the ‘Scope’ of the call (Topic identifier: DT-FOF-05-2019)  
We focus on a key consumer goods sector (FMCG). The OI B2B/B2C digital platform and fablabs will couple design, creativity, and knowledge sharing with a customer-driven production approach. Our approach allows for the involvement of customers and the entire value chain in the co-creation of products. We will develop and promote the adoption of collaborative production engineering approaches (OI B2B/B2C digital platform and fablabs) in the FMCG sector. This will spur the rapid production and commercialisation of new/innovative customised FMCG for the benefit of consumers. We cover the following areas highlighted in scope:  
- Novel approaches to capitalise on the knowledge and ideas of design and engineering coming from different and even new actors: The Open Innovation B2B/B2C digital platform is a knowledge sharing and knowledge management platform integrated with digital and business process modelling tools (formulation design, formulation modelling and performance simulation and advanced data analytics including cognitive, ontological, social, multi-parametric analysis, etc.) that allow the whole value chain including consumers and new actors to digitally design and simulate the performance of new formulated powdered/liquid products.  
- Design of new strategies based on creative and agile methodologies for analysis: Corroboration of formulation performance against simulation data with in-process and final product characterization data from fablabs/MDFs will be used to optimise the formulation models and develop fablab process digital twin. This will progressively enhance the robustness and performance of the digital platform. Real-time analysis of demand and feedback from consumers and visibility of such data across the value chain will facilitate agile responses from the innovation ecosystem in meeting the customers’ evolving needs. Consumer feedback will help us design high-quality ‘customized/personalised products. Analytical data tools such as heat mapping and A/B testing will be used for real-time analysis of User Experience and to determine the most effective. Real-time user feedback and satisfaction ratings on the features of our platform and fablab will be used to optimise the digital platform and fablab.  
- Development of open source product data exchange and standard representations of products and processes that ensure the compatibility of modelling and simulation with different process information systems: Information on formulation ingredients/products and processes will be curated and displayed in a consistent digital format, allowing compatibility of modelling and simulation with different process information systems. Our platform will have the ability to ‘plug-in’ users’ models and protect the related IP. Simulation of new customized/personalised formulated product capabilities will allow validation of compliance to safety and regulatory standards [e.g. EU Detergents Regulation (EC) No 648/2004]. We will comply with EU General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) to ensure the security of users’ data and use tools developed by our partners to ensure the same. Adequate IP and business models will be implemented to ensure no conflicts arise between manufacturers and suppliers providing components for a ‘plug & produce’ platform and fablab. An open platform based upon a DLT/blockchain-secured data exchange layer will allow manufacturers, suppliers, new FMCG sector actors (e.g. entrepreneurs, SMEs, start-ups), and consumers to collaborate in a way that makes their transactions secure, trustworthy, and transparent. For very sensitive information such as prices, partial homomorphic encryption techniques will be used to perform transparent verifications without disclosing the records. Moreover, the deployment of open source tools will promote the open exchange, collaborative participation, and transparency.  
- Development of new Manufacturing Demonstration Facilities (MDFs), where we will test new technologies in cooperation with fablabs and makers in order to develop real industrial products and where training is offered: STEF and CPI will host the developed fablabs and act as open access MDFs, offering fablab manufacturing services to individuals and companies (especially SMEs) using our digital platform to design new customised/personalised products. The MDFs will also offer training to companies and individuals looking to adopt the new DIY formulation design and manufacturing approach.  
- Integration of Social Science and Humanities (SSH) aspects regarding creativity: SSH aspects regarding creativity are at the heart of our project as we aim to empower consumers and new actors to participate in the creation and manufacture of new customised/personalised FMCG addressing societal needs. We will analyse the (wide public) human-DIY processes acceptance and define best practices to increase awareness about positive impacts of the DIY products and creativity. Examples of societal drivers for DIY and personalisation/customisation include cost reduction, health reasons, preference for natural ingredients, reduced chemical use, and environmental protection. Moreover, legal expertise is involved in our consortium to tackle issues of digital security, data protection, and privacy by design.  
- Facilitating cooperation with other projects: This will be achieved by leveraging our involvement in other national and international research and innovation activities linked to our project (section 1.3.5). Cooperation with other projects not involving any consortium partner will be achieved via Outreach activities (section 2.2) such as the EFFRA events.  
- Enhancing user involvement: via OI digital platform and open access MDFs; upselling and willingness business strategies; creation of an open community & multilingual platform; and royalties for the best shared formulations.  
- Ensuring the accessibility and reusability of data produced during our project: Defined in Data Management Plan, use of DLT-based records and Research Data Storage Facility (section 2.2a.4)  
TRL: Our activities will start at TRL4 and achieve TRL6 at the end of the project (section 1.3.3).  
Project cost: We are requesting an EC contribution of 5,986,276€, which is in the range specified in the scope.  
Fit to the ‘Expected Impact’ of the call (Topic identifier: DT-FOF-05-2019)  
Establish Open-Innovation networks for manufacturing that support customer-driven production all around Europe: delivered by our digital platform and open access fablab MDFs  
Creation of specific business models for the engineering of customised solutions, particularly for SMEs, rapid demand changes, and shorter time to market: The development of specific business models for our outputs is a key objective of our project (sections 1.1 and 3.1) and will be delivered in WP2 (section 3).  
Improvement of the co-design and co-development capabilities towards a reduction of development costs of new products and services: Our co-design and co-development digital platform and fablab MDFs for new customised/personalised FMCG will reduce development time/cost and make small-scale manufacture of customised/personalised FMCG economically attractive.  
Increase of product variety and personalisation for higher customer satisfaction and loyalty: The digital platform and fablab will allow ‘plug and play’/DIY design and rapid manufacture of new personalised/customized formulations meeting individual consumer preferences.

Work programme Call - FACTORIES OF THE FUTURE - FOF  

Expectation from Work programme OLEDSOLAR Project addressing the expectations  
The Factories of the Future Public-Private Partnership (PPP) initiative aims at helping EU manufacturing enterprises, SMEs, to adapt to global competitive pressures by developing the necessary key enabling technologies to support EU manufacturing across a broad range of sectors. It will help European industry to meet the increasing global consumer demand for greener, more customised and higher quality products through the necessary transition to a flexible, digitalised and demand-driven industry with lower waste generation and energy consumption.  
Our project is in line with the FOF PPP initiative as it is aimed at improving the competitiveness of the manufacturing industry involved in the development of opto-electronic devices e.g. OLEDs, OPVs and CIGS by offering and developing enhanced manufacturing processes, improved metrology and automated inline measurement, control and inspection systems capable of enhancing production yield and analysing 100% of defects during the production of components at production rate. Our project will help the EU industrial sector to meet consumer demand in terms of high-quality manufacturing, 100% reliable and efficient products and components with minimum waste generation and efficient resource utilisation.  
Call Topic: DT-FOF-03-2018: Innovative manufacturing of opto-electrical parts  
Topic Challenge How we address the Challenges  
New adjustable processes for scaled up production of free form and complex opto-electronic parts and devices and equipment for testing, failure analysis and control using a range of sensors.  
We propose manufacturing processes, inspection techniques and tools, automation through a sensors network and recycling processes development that will address the challenge of opto-electronic parts and devices manufacturing, process adjustment as per devices customisation and specification needs and defect-free manufacturing of such parts in production through inline monitoring.  
Scope of the topic How we address the scope?  
New, flexible, and innovative process chains to handle complex designs that include opto-electrical functionalities.  
Our project proposes innovative manufacturing processes including automated printing techniques for OPVs allowing free form module production and devices customisation. Further, innovative deposition techniques we propose will improve CIGS and OLEDs devices performance and efficiency by enabling enhanced barrier film performance, and the proposed inline inspection and testing techniques (photoluminescence, optical diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, spectroscopic ellipsometry) that will result in defect-free production of OLEDs, OPVs and CIGS devices.  
Improved sensor equipment for quality control in the different processing steps as well as the final functionality of the component.  
We will implement an improved and optimised network of sensors and testing equipment for quality control and monitoring of different manufacturing processes of OLEDs and solar cells.  
Methodologies for improving quality through high-precision automation using the sensor data, including non-destructive in-process evaluation of material and functional component properties.  
Our proposed methodologies will allow precise automation enabled by sensors and intelligent decision management software for the production of OLEDs and solar cells within R2R and S2S production environments. For NDT and in-process evaluation of material and functional properties of OLEDs and solar cells during production; development of hybrid inspection and measurement techniques and systems will be optimised in our project including Photoluminescence, optical diffraction, shearography, thermography, Raman spectroscopy, Spectroscopic ellipsometry, HST and OCT etc.  
Re-use and requalification of key components and precious materials within the process chain from products at their end of life.  
Recycling and re-use approaches will be developed in our project allowing more than 40% of defected parts to be reused and recycled.

1.2 Relation to the work programme  

Our project addresses topic ICT-37-2020 Advancing photonics technologies and application driven photonics components and the innovation ecosystem: Sub-topic Flexible Farm-to-Fork Sensing. Table 2 outlines how we fit to the specific topic challenge and scope, and to other relevant parts of the “Towards 2020: Photonics Driving Economic Growth in Europe – Multiannual Strategic Roadmap 2014-2020”.  

Table 2: Contribution of our project to the ICT-37-2020 call and the Photonic Strategy Roadmap  

Challenge I as defined in ICT-37-2020 call  
"To develop and apply core photonics technology for the next generation of devices (including components, modules and sub-systems) in order to drive innovation in key application areas, which are significant for current or future markets and where photonics can bring a key competitive advantage."  
Our project contribution  
We will design, develop and validate next-generation MIR photonic sensing devices, based on innovative MIR light emitting diode (IC-LED) light sources and ICL/QCL lasers combined with novel waveguides, for the detection of microbial and chemical contamination in food and commodities along the food value chain.  

Challenge II as defined in ICT-37-2020 call  
"Innovative photonic sensing solutions can contribute to reducing food production losses and food wastes, estimated in Europe at about 300 kg per capita, and to increasing food safety for the end consumer along the food production chain from farm to fork."  
Our project contribution  
We will provide more and better data on contamination along the food production chain, since on-site monitoring data from the MI-FI device and reference data from the HI-FI device will be connected to existing data platforms such as the Food Trust™. The use of our data about microbial and chemical contamination will in future allow detecting the weak points of the food chain. Thus, we will make a considerable contribution to the reduction of food wastes, where the reduction of wastes in cereals production and import of nuts and dry fruits will be about 40% and 60% in aquaponic herbs production of the existing losses associated with the fungal and mycotoxin contaminations.  

Scope I as defined in ICT-37-2020 call  
"Development of an innovative smart photonic sensor solution, utilizing an appropriate bandwidth between the ultraviolet (UV) and the far-infrared spectral range for monitoring food quality with respect to microbiological and chemical contamination along the farm-to-fork food production chain."  
Our project contribution  
We will provide the first low-cost hand-held MIR sensing solution (MI-FI) and a high-end portable MIR device (HI-FI) enabling flexible microbial and chemical analysis of food products on farms, in food processing, wholesale and retail. The MIR region was chosen as the appropriate bandwidth since it has proven to be the most discriminative region for determination of chemical and microbial contaminants. The photonics sensing solution will contain an innovative smart paper-based microfluidic sampling pre-concentration allowing the detection of tiny amounts of contaminants.  

Scope II as defined in ICT-37-2020 call  
"The targeted solution should combine photonic sensing technology with advanced data analysis techniques and be portable, easy-to-use, flexible, and broadly adaptable for usage on farms, in food processing, wholesale and retail."  
Our project contribution  
We will innovatively combine the developed photonic sensing devices with advanced data analysis approaches for mining specific fungal and mycotoxin contaminants in the pre-concentrated and analyte-separated samples. We will develop robust calibration modelling strategies for the data obtained by our proposed photonic solutions using the most advanced pre-processing, machine learning and deep learning tools. The calibration models for the cases demonstrated in our project will serve as a template for further developments beyond the project period as they will set standards in the data pre-processing and methodology to detect and utilize specific molecular signatures. The models will be established in a cloud service and provided as an application for a laptop (HI-FI device), and as a mobile app (MI-FI device). We will further develop a strategy for data integration into existing platforms such as the Food Trust™ Block Chain. The developed technology will be validated and demonstrated for usage on farms, food processing, wholesale and retail through involvement of the relevant partners.  

Scope III as defined in ICT-37-2020 call  
"Actions should focus on the following areas: (1) food production by small/medium-sized farms; (2) novel types of food production, such as aquaponics; (3) on-site food processing and vending (e.g. on farms or local food markets)."  
Our project contribution  
We focus on (1) small/medium-sized cereal farms through NEBIH, nuts and dried fruit production farms through the distributor Seeberger; (2) novel type aquaponic and on-site food producer BIGH. We will perform extensive demonstration of HI-FI and MI-FI devices by Seeberger, end-user ROMER, food-safety authority NEBIH and aquaponics company BIGH. As a food safety authority, NEBIH has direct contact with more than 3000 small and medium-sized farms in Hungary, therefore our photonic solution can be tested under various field conditions.  

Strategic Objective as defined in Photonic Strategic Roadmap  
"The European photonics industry must now continue working with the European Commission and national policy makers to coordinate a joint approach to innovation, and to pool investments for enabling the rapid development of new products and minimising times to market. This to market approach needs to include the entire value chain, from advanced research through to technology take-up, pilot lines, and manufacturing platforms."  
Our project contribution  
Our consortium represents the entire “photonics value chain”, from advanced research partners (UULM, BOKU, WU, NMBU) to component manufacturers (NP), systems engineering and manufacturing companies (HS, IRIS), laboratory analytics service providers (ROMER), end-users among food businesses (BAMA, BIGH, Seeberger), food control authorities (NEBIH), and provider of large-scale integrated traceability solutions (IBM in advisory role). Customer and stakeholder involvement, prototype development, pilot production, on-site validation, end-user testing, market scanning and further business development can be seamlessly coordinated in our consortium, ensuring minimal time-to-market and rapid scale-up.

Figure 15 VirtualLab™ interference simulation of a single mode laser Vs an M2=9 laser To utilize an M2=30 source for DLIP, we need to improve the coherence of the beam. The beam quality factor M4 is defined as M4 = M2 M2, the product of the beam quality factors in both Cartesian axes. In general, M4 can only be increased, and is the equivalent to entropy in optical systems. However, using beam steering and shaping, we can decrease the M2 value in one axis while increasing it in the other. This creates a rectangle with a very coherent narrow dimension and a high non-coherent wide dimension. Such a rectangle can then be shaped and split to interfere with itself along the narrow axis, giving highly efficient interference, which is suitable for 2 beams DLIP writing. Figure 16 Far-field Multimode laser beam A^2 SumA^2 25 A Far-field Multimode laser beam can be depicted as a grid of rectangular segments, with the x or y linear dimension of each rectangle equal to the beam diameter at e-2 divided by M2 value in the corresponding x or y axis, as shown in Figure 16. Each rectangle’s wave front is close to zero (in terms of geometrical representation), or close to a constant phase value in complex field representation. Thus, its optical characteristics are as close to a pure single mode Gaussian beam as possible. Although each segment has a non-Gaussian amplitude, the focused spot of each segment (sub-beam) would be very similar to a Gaussian one – the Sinc function (the Fourier transform of a plane wave). By utilizing advanced optics, we can re-arrange the rectangles so that the beam is transformed into a line with asymmetric M2, with the y axis having almost single mode coherence properties. After the sub-beams are re-arranged, a line diffuser is usually applied in the long axis to achieve a uniform line. State of the art refractive methods used to transform M2 suffer from many limitations, including: High NA causing high sensitivity to alignment, internal foci, and multiple thick elements adding significant temporal dispersion to ultra-short pulses. Our novel M2 transformation concept is based on a unique diffractive technology, enabling a low NA system that is robust to alignment and has thin fused silica elements that do not cause large temporal dispersion. Figure 17 Current HOLO-OR laboratory demonstrator 532nm Our design will consist of 2 modules: the 1st accepts the laser beam from the fiber and has an M2 transformed output line. The second module (designed in cooperation with FRAUNHOFER) takes this beam and collimates the line and then splits it in the narrow axis and projects the split lines to a focal region, as shown in Figure 18. The proposed optical system will combine the high-power output of the low coherence multi-mode ps laser with the precision of high coherence interference patterning, by transforming the beam coherence properties utilizing a novel, robust and optically thin diffractive method. Figure 18 M2 transformation The main challenges to be addressed by us are defined as: • Laser power damage thresholds: Depending on laser beam size, the extremely high-power density would require some special design consideration including beam expansion and working with larger than typical components. • Effect of M2 transformation on interference modulation is unknown, as this is usually done to focus a single beam into a narrow line, not to interfere it. • High efficiency Transformation: At least 85% of input beam power needs to be in the transformed beam. Production process development and optimization for the diffractive component is required. In-line inspection process – Advance beyond the state of art: A surface inspection system for ultrahigh throughput patterning will be proposed and tested. A simplified, rugged and vibration immune optical configuration will be integrated in the processing head (combined off-axis multi-illumination with coaxial optical information gathering) to provide sub-micron resolution identification of critical pattern parameters. High frequency sampling will contribute to reducing vibration influences and other noise sources. It will be optimized for the target height range and required resolution. 26 Samples characterization will allow defining best optical interferometric techniques and configuration to reach expected resolution. Our optics sampling strategy will be defined with partners for reducing samples and accelerating data transmission. Fast CCD sensor technology, such as lock-in cameras featuring pixel level synchronous demodulation, will be considered for integration for increasing acquisition speed and processing of data. Algorithms will be developed and programmed for extracting (inference) height information from microtopography and simplifying data array to be sent to laser controller (height value for x-y position, and possible defects, as well as hints of process deviations). High communication rate with laser head controller will be implemented. Regarding the coherent light measurement system, the challenge to progress beyond the state of the art will be to effectively implement diffractometry for inline process monitoring. This will require the design of compact, high numerical aperture optics to capture the maximum number of orders possible. Similar optical systems have been used to apply sub-wavelength optical diffraction metrology to characterize structures as small as 50 nm in periodic patterns. As such, we will start by calibrating the system on parts of known geometries with several patterns required for obtaining the effects for each application. Variations of the basic system may be developed, having adjusted light sources and optics to the resolution and spot size needed, and for compatibility with flat surfaces and non-flat surfaces. These will be integrated in the related production lines to provide dimensional information on the structures in real time. Resulting data can be used to feed the processing models where appropriate. Regarding scatterometry and image-based inspection, the main challenge is the use of a single matrix sensor with adequate wavefront shaping and filtering, to gather the 2D scattering function of the surface being patterned and extract relevant information on the pattern homogeneity, quality and process deviations, at the high processing speed imposed by our unique PROMETHEUS DLIP process. This will be done combining precisely timed lock-in loops of illumination and snapshot image capture (coaxial to the laser incidence), and heavily relying on dedicated on-board hardware processing with programmable architectures: cascaded Field-Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs) and General-Purpose Graphic Processing Units (GPGPUs). On-line close-loop operation and process digitalization: Using the online measurement and imaging systems developed, it will be possible for us to adapt the laser focal spot, sync and power, as well as correcting the laser optical chain in real time, to respond to variabilities in workpiece behavior and light-matter interactions, to optimize the overall production yield. A global interface will allow for communication between the different tools in the production chain: sensors, process parameters, laser and optical components of the processing heads. The whole data for a complete production chain will be available for process monitoring and optimization. Different sensors, readout electronics (digitalization) and data managing strategies are considered for the three main types of process sensors considered: laser beam sensors, active scatterometry, and image-based inspection tools. As control algorithms not only standard PID-control algorithms will be implemented and evaluated, but also more advanced control approaches (neural networks) will be evaluated, in the case the performance of the PID-control algorithms does not meet the system specifications (in particular, productivity). So far Single Input Single Output (SISO) controllers have been discussed, which is a relevant approach in the case signals from several sensors are “fused” into one input signal for the controller. As several sensor signals (inputs) are sampled during laser processing, and more than one process output can be identified, allows us to control the processes on the basis of a Multi Input Multi Output (MIMO) algorithm, or Multi Input Single Output (MISO) algorithm. This is particularly relevant for DLIP, where a multiplicity of geometrical variables in the optical chain affect in a combined and coordinated way the various characteristics of the resulting energy distribution pattern to be adjusted. 27 Fibre delivery - Advance beyond the state of the art: We will address the current issues relating to fibre delivery of the high-power lasers through innovative new fiber designs: • The first approach will be an extension of current work performed internally. Specially designed hollow core fiber based on a single ring of anti-resonant elements (AREs) surrounding a central hollow core will be designed. Thanks to the signal propagation in the air hollow-core fibers are a versatile optical transmission medium. They are used in a large number of applications thanks to their low non-linearity, tailorable dispersion, and the freedom to control the composition and pressure of gases in the core. Fiber design will have to be optimized for the MW power project goal and in particular improving the fiber injection to prevent fiber end facet damage. • The second approach relies on multicore technology. Our technical staff brings experienced personnel recognized in Spatial Division Multiplexing records capacity transmission in multimode as well as single mode MCF. This approach offers the opportunity to mutualize peak power among several cores. This enables us to increase fiber damage threshold as power is divided among N cores and offers the possibility to tailor the fiber design to suit best the application in particular beam shape at the output. Considering that 100 W nanosecond pulse as a potential limit per core, we expect a 12-core fiber geometry could meet the end goal project. 1.2 Relation to the BBI-JU Annual Work Plan  
Our proposal, addressing the topic BBI -2019-SO2-R3, Apply microorganisms and/or enzymes to resolve end-of-life issues of plastics, will contribute significantly to the KPIs set out in the Strategic Innovation and Research Agenda (SIRA) 6 and is fully aligned with the BBI roadmap as will be further discussed in section 2. Our overriding goal aligns with most of its strategic orientations (SO) (fig. 1.2): SO1) The possibility to biodegrade plastic used in agriculture will make the crops growing more sustainable and significantly reduce the impact of land use and agriculture practices for biomass production; SO2) the core of our activities will deal with the setup of an efficient bio-technological (using enzymes, microorganisms optionally vectorised into insects) multistep process to convert fossil plastics into new valuable products; SO3) such as fertilizers, bioplastics and edible coatings materials which will be validated by relevant industry end users to reach SO 4. Besides creating socio-economic value, our project will also help the EU in implementing better waste management strategies as well as reducing the major societal challenge reflected in the European Plastics strategy. We further cover the following cross-cutting KETs: industrial biotechnology is at its core, advanced materials and manufacturing systems (converting waste into new bio-based feedstocks such as chitin/chitosan with support from advanced tools such as online monitoring and modeling). As part of our multidisciplinary approach, socio-economic science and humanities will also be considered where relevant, e.g. through the performing of a social-LCA, studying economics, sustainability as well as ethics aspects but also including marketing, policy making, training and upskilling in WP6-8 and also by getting public feedback from the dissemination and communication activities. The detailed relation of our proposal to the specific challenges and scope addressed by the topic are outlined in Table 1.1 below:

Table 1.1: How our project relates to the scope and challenges of the BBI 2019.SO2.R3 topic  
Topic text How our project addresses these challenges/scope  
Include tailored microorganisms and/or enzymes for co-digestion or co-fermentation of different feedstock, provided that the developed solutions result in sufficient yields in terms of: (i) breaking down the feedstock; and/or (ii) conversion into valuable products.  
We use previously independently tested enzymes, microorganisms and insects that have shown the potential to degrade plastics (see details in section 1.4). The core proposal innovation lies on their novel combination aimed at maximizing the yield of the decomposition (insects) and degradation (microorganisms and enzymes). This will be achieved through the vectorisation of the later within the insects among other combined approaches to be tested. Earthworms are also incorporated in the compost as a synergistic agent to reach the ambitious proposal goal. The composting will be optimised possibly in presence of other biodegradable feedstocks (WP4). Furthermore, once our biodegradation agents have finished their lifetime, the shell of the insects will be used to extract chitin, in turn converting a complementary fraction of the compost into new biobased materials available for further processing.

To biodegrade/decompose waste streams containing non-biodegradable polymers […] where standard EoL/eco-design principles cannot be applied. May even be applied to all sorts of residual streams without any preliminary separation or sorting operations.  
We focus on fossil-based plastics used in agricultural and packaging sectors. Those mainly include PE, PET, PP, PS, EVA, etc. as well as laminates thereof among which at least 5 representative materials will be selected in WP1. Those are generally not bio-based, no intrinsically biodegradable and currently have a very low recycling rate (due to difficult recyclability in view of their fouling and/or complex structures). Our biotechnological degradation and valorisation process will be tuned to match the requirements of each of the selected materials employing or combining several approaches.  
We will first establish our process on separated plastics (sorted, and verifying the absence of contaminants that could affect the process) such as agricultural films which composition is generally known at the source as well as on selected food packaging. Based on the results, in WP6, we will also provide recommendations on how the case of comprising several materials can be approached (e.g. combining several enzymes). Cascade processing will also be discussed for that purpose. In the long term, possibly performing further development for fractions not covered in our project, especially in case of actual MSW streams having very complex composition, it could be possible to apply a similar process without pre-sorting even of the MSW or for agri-waste of mixed organic and plastic types.  
Apply the circular economy concept to plastics. Avoid plastic littering land and sea and reduce plastic waste diverted to landfill or incineration.  
Finding value-added EoL for plastics which are currently landfilled or incinerated (with a cost), or even far too often left on the soil, can act as the actual incentive towards the adoption of processes like ours and significantly curb the current alarming figures. The potential revenues generated along the value chain, further discussed in section 2.2, support the way towards circular economy. This can help converting AWP to a feedstock with a value but also generate different products that can have their own markets. All in all, this will allow meeting the growing society’s and users’ demands to improve the EoL options to avoid landfilling and support manufacturers, who are responsible for the suitability of the materials they commercialise, to comply with relevant waste legislations.  
During our project implementation, benchmarking will be done vs. current EoL (often limited to landfilling or incineration) including economic as well as environmental sustainability studies of the delivered processes.  
Remove non-biodegradable polymers from the environment.  
Besides the plastics entering waste management systems that will be treated in novel industrial composting processes, our solutions will also be suitable for reducing the amount of non-biodegradable plastics reaching the environment in 2 ways: i) the in situ bioremediation of the agricultural plastics left on the soil will be extensively studied in field trials applying vermicompost based on a tuned combination of insects, enzymes and microorganisms (such an approach could also in the long term be adjusted as contingency for accidental littering in highly contaminated areas); ii) microplastics removal from compost, a critical problem for the current organic waste management companies, due to the presence of non-biodegradable plastic contaminants not properly sorted in the supposedly organic fraction collected from e.g. MSW.  
Result in new feedstock for the bio-based industry.  
In contrast, we will convert non-biobased AWP into a valuable feedstock for the BBI, and we will generate a number of semi-finished and finished value-added bio-enabled products to be commercialised across and beyond our value chain of actors such as tailored:  
- enzymes  
- microorganisms  
- insects & earthworms  
- vermicompost  
- fertilizers from by-products of our process  
- chitin and chitosan from by-products from our process with demonstrated potential to be used as new bioplastic packaging and to enhance mulching films and agriculture materials (pots, tips, etc.).  
The TRL at the end of our project should be 4-5 for the bio-based value chain in question. Clearly state the starting and end TRLs of the key technologies.  
The start, progress and end TRL of each technical component is justified in table 1.4. After characterizing the select AWP, we will work subsequently at lab (WP2) and pilot scale (WP3-5 covering the relevant steps along our value chain) obtaining significant amounts of biotechnological material for an industrially relevant validation of the new process. Different techno-economical aspects of scalability will be considered in WP6 to give recommendations for successful transfer of the different steps of our process to a sustainable industrial scale, so the results TRL is further matured to reach the market.  
Industry participation in our project would be considered as an added value because it can play a supportive role to demonstrate the potential for integrating the developed concepts and technologies into current industrial landscapes or existing plants so they can be deployed more quickly and scaled up to apply industry-wide.  
The whole circular bio-based value chain is covered by our partners. All WPs involve industrial partners, e.g. in the supply of AWP based on the initial industrial requirements defined in WP1, in the supply of enzymes and insects in WP2, in the upscaling of the biotechnological solutions for degrading plastics in WP3, in the scale up of both in situ and ex situ degradation trials, in the conversion/validation of the final value-added product in WP5, as well as in the final sustainability and feasibility assessments (WP6). Our consortium has a strong industry component including the participation of 7 SMEs, 3 Large enterprises and many more associated via membership in the participating industry association. It is noteworthy that 2 partners are associated BIC members and therefore committed to making the BBI roadmap successful. We cover the agricultural, food, plastics, fertilizer, biotech, etc. sectors. This dense ecosystem will allow researchers and early adopters alike to cooperate, understand the specific market demands, and set the basis for the long-term success of the new value chains. They will also drive the knowledge transfer across all involved sectors

1.2 Methodology Overall methodology, including concepts, models and assumptions: We follow a circular systems methodology (Figure 1) integrating molecular design, industrial processing, digital intelligence and validated end-of-life recovery within a closed innovation loop. Figure 1. High-level schematic of the NIPULOOP concept This approach is implemented as a circular NIPU technology platform linking material design, processing, digital control and end-of-life recovery across the value chain. As illustrated in the schematic, we structure the project as a continuous circular flow linking feedstocks, material design, manufacturing, end-use validation and closed-loop recovery, and it is supported by QbD, digital intelligence and exploitation pathways. Circular-by-design NIPU chemistry, guided by SSbD principles, is developed from renewable and secondary feedstocks (including bio-based, CO₂-derived and waste-derived inputs) and systematically engineered through structure–property–process understanding, and optimised using AI-driven modelling and process analytics. We integrate formulations into existing industrial manufacturing lines through process-integrated scale-up (TRL5) supported by the earlier QbD learnings, Industry 4.0 PAT and inline monitoring, enabling robust pilot-scale production. This includes compatibility with established PU processing technologies (e.g. RIM, RTM, coatings and reactive extrusion), ensuring industrial relevance and uptake. We validate these materials in ≥4 TRL6 mobility demonstrators from among the UCs (discussed under Cluster 3), positioned at the core of the value chain and directly linked to end-user requirements and regulatory constraints. At end-of-life, we selectively recover materials, chemically depolymerise and re-valorise them into cyclic carbonates and amines, including recovery from both designed NIPU materials and secondary waste streams (e.g. PU/CDW-derived fractions), which we reintegrate into new NIPU formulations, thereby closing the material loop and demonstrating full circularity at the system level. This circular flow will be supported by a digital twin and FAIR data framework, enabling traceability, optimisation and interoperability across all stages, including integration with the Common Data Platform for Chemicals and DPP concepts. We perform circular business models, techno-economic and lifecycle assessments, standardisation and regulatory compliance alongside the technical development to ensure economic viability, industrial scalability and post-project deployment. In order to deliver impact and prepare for post-project continuation, which will require further scale-up of the results, our methodology is also supported by the development of a solid business case (including market research, cost competitiveness, Return-on-Investment, value proposition, value chain requirements, barriers and enablers), go-to-market and exploitation strategies, skills development and cross-sector replication pathways. By integrating advanced materials development with digital intelligence and circular business models, we establish a fully digitalised circular materials workflow (applicable across key material configurations (e.g. foams, coatings and elastomeric systems), positioning Europe at the forefront of safe, circular and data-driven polymer research and development and manufacturing, reducing dependency on imported isocyanates and fossil-based intermediates while strengthening resilient and sustainable industrial value chains. Methodological Framework for achieving our project’s objectives: We structure NIPULOOP around 6 interlinked technological and innovation clusters that together enable the full innovation cycle from circular material design through industrial integration to system-level circular validation across strategic value chains. These clusters are strongly interconnected, with continuous feedback loops between material design, processing, digital intelligence and recycling, ensuring that circularity, safety and industrial feasibility are systematically reinforced throughout the project and collectively implemented as a coherent circular NIPU technology platform (operationalised through the work plan presented in Section 3.1). Circular-by-Design NIPU Chemistry (TRL4–5): This cluster establishes the chemical and material foundations of NIPULOOP through a circular-by-design approach, in which we address feedstock selection, molecular architecture, processability and end-of-life recovery as a single, integrated design problem. Our methodology combines renewable and secondary feedstock conversion, formulation design, and design-for-recycling criteria, ensuring that performance, safety and circularity are embedded from the outset. We align material formulations with the functional and regulatory requirements of target applications, particularly for the mobility sector. Feedstock development and qualification: Our approach will start with the preparation and qualification of renewable and secondary feedstocks, with particular focus on used cooking oil (UCO), CO₂-derived intermediates, and secondary fractions recovered from construction and demolition waste (CDW), including PU-derived amine-rich streams. We follow a controlled, multi-stage conversion route from waste-derived lipid streams to functional cyclic carbonate precursors suitable for NIPU synthesis. This includes purification, epoxidation and carbonation steps, each optimised to ensure reproducibility, chemical functionality and compatibility with downstream processing. We integrate feedstock variability as a core methodological parameter in formulation and process development. Variations in fatty acid composition, impurity profiles and seasonal supply are systematically characterised, as these directly influence epoxide functionality, cyclic carbonate yield and ultimately NIPU network structure. We optimise reaction conditions for epoxidation and carbonation to maximise functional group conversion while limiting side reactions, with target yields ≥85% at each stage. The resulting intermediates are fully characterised using spectroscopic and thermal techniques, ensuring traceability between feedstock composition and material performance. We assess CDW-derived fractions as secondary feedstock sources, focusing on recovery of functional intermediates (e.g. amines) compatible with NIPU synthesis. In parallel, we carry out a supply chain and traceability analysis to assess availability, certification requirements, price stability and regulatory status of all primary feedstocks, including UCO, CO₂ streams, bio-based amines, crosslinkers, and secondary raw materials recovered from CDW streams. We quantify bio-based and recycled carbon content according to relevant standards (e.g. ASTM D6866, EN 16785, EN 16640, ISO 16620), providing a consistent basis for downstream sustainability assessment. This ensures that feedstocks entering formulation are not only chemically suitable but also industrially viable and compliant. This includes integration of CDW-derived secondary feedstocks, linking upstream waste recovery with downstream material design and processing. Formulation design and structure–property–process integration: We develop NIPU materials through aminolysis of cyclic carbonate precursors with bio-based amines and oligomeric crosslinkers, using a systematic exploration of molecular design space. Our methodology explicitly links molecular architecture (crosslink density, segment composition, functional group distribution) to both performance targets and processability constraints. Formulation development is guided by application-driven requirements, particularly in the mobility sector, where materials must meet defined mechanical, thermal and durability criteria while remaining compatible with industrial processes such as reactive extrusion and moulding. Our material development covers complementary configurations including coatings, thermoformable systems, textile coatings/binders and structural NIPU elements aligned with the targeted mobility applications. Across all formulations, we target bio-based and/or recycled carbon content ≥70%. The NIPULOOP approach is structured around application-driven material development aligned with the defined mobility use cases (Cluster 3). Our formulation, processing and validation activities are guided by the requirements of these use cases, covering coatings, thermoformed components, textile systems and structural elements. This ensures that our material development remains directly linked to real application constraints, industrial processing routes and regulatory requirements, while maintaining consistency across formulations, processing strategies and demonstrator validation pathways. Our formulation strategy follows an iterative approach, with an initial screening phase exploring a broad compositional space to identify structure–property relationships, followed by a refinement phase in which formulations are progressively constrained by recyclability criteria, safety screening (SSbD) and preliminary processing feedback. This will avoid late-stage redesign and ensures that only materials meeting combined performance, processability and circularity requirements progress further. We generate experimental datasets (conversion behaviour, mechanical performance, thermal stability, rheology) using standardised analytical methods and structure them according to FAIR principles, enabling their use in modelling and digital frameworks in subsequent clusters. Design-for-recycling and circularity integration: We build recyclability into the material design from the start and develop the formulations within defined limits on network structure, functionality and additive use, so that depolymerisation and reuse remain feasible under realistic conditions. This is achieved through a parallel evaluation of end-of-life pathways applicable to NIPU systems, including chemical depolymerisation (hydrolysis, aminolysis), thermomechanical reprocessing based on dynamic covalent bond exchange, and mechanical recycling. For each route, we define the material characteristics required to achieve target recovery performance, including crosslink density, bond reversibility, functional group accessibility and additive compatibility. We translate these requirements into formulation guidelines that specify acceptable ranges for monomer functionality, network architecture and additive use. Constraints such as the exclusion of substances that interfere with depolymerisation or contaminate recovered streams are applied early in the design process. Only formulations meeting minimum recyclability criteria are advanced, ensuring alignment between material design and downstream recovery processes. We further formalise circularity criteria through quantitative descriptors (e.g. topology freezing temperature, activation energies for bond exchange, gel fraction thresholds) that are structured for integration into modelling and digital frameworks. This will enable simultaneous optimisation of performance, processability and recyclability, avoiding trade-offs typically encountered when circularity is treated as a secondary objective. We will carry out preliminary validation of reprocessability through controlled reprocessing cycles, ensuring that materials designed for circularity retain mechanical integrity & compatibility with industrial processing conditions. 1.2 Methodology Overall methodology, including concepts, models and assumptions Concept: We designed CO2HESION to bridge the gap between high-temperature carbon capture and high-value chemical synthesis, ensuring a TRL increase from 4 to 6 through a structured four-step data-driven engineering cycle HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products Page 11 of 48 linking: 1) component optimisation; 2) multivariable modelling and interface engineering; 3) system integration and Digital Twin synchronisation; 4) industrial demonstration and end-user certification. Each stage is mapped to a dedicated Technical Work Package (WP 1-WP6), ensuring that our engineering leadership at TRSA is harmonized with the scientific validation provided by UNIZAR and INEGI, while directly addressing the Market Pull from ORL and TOR. The fundamental challenge in conventional CCU is the massive energy penalty associated with the "logistics of CO2", specifically the compression, liquefaction, and transport of purified gas. We disrupt this by positioning the direct integration of V-CaL capture with downstream conversion as a process intensification paradigm. By utilising a Hub-and-Spoke architecture, we eliminate the need for intermediate phase changes, utilising the thermal and chemical state of the captured CO2 directly from the source. This concept is realised via 2 distinct process routes (as illustrated in Figure 1): Route 1: Thermocatalytic Synthesis of Low-Carbon Olefins: Our first route focuses on the production of ethylene (C2H4) and propylene (C3H6) through the integration of the patented CSF reactor with a 25 kW AEM electrolyzer as a green hydrogen source. The thermodynamic novelty here lies in the Equilibrium-Breaking nature of the Continuous Sorbent Feeding system. • Overcoming Thermodynamic Limits: Traditional Reverse Water Gas Shift (RWGS) and subsequent oligomerisation are inhibited by the H2O byproduct, which poisons catalysts and shifts equilibrium backward. The CSF reactor utilises a proprietary sorbent system that continuously removes moisture in-situ, shifting the reaction with an increase of up to 100% in CO2 conversion per pass. • Energy Recovery: By coupling the exothermic nature of the olefin synthesis with the heat requirements of the V-CaL desorber, we achieve a >40% reduction in total energy intensity. • Industrial Validation: The resulting olefin streams are pressurized and fed to ORL for "drop-in" testing, proving that renewable-powered chemistry can meet the stringent purity standards of the petrochemical sector. Route 2: Mineralisation for Circular Lithium Carbonate: Our second route demonstrates a direct mineralisation pathway that bypasses the need for hydrogen altogether, focusing on the ceramic industry’s "closed-loop" potential. ■Chemical Synergy: Purified CO2 from the V-CaL hub is reacted with lithium-bearing precursors (sourced and pre-processed by ACC from battery recycling) to synthesize Li2CO3. ■Eliminating Purification Extremes: This route eliminates the need for the "ultra-purification" steps required for sensitive hydrogenation catalysts. The mineralisation process is inherently more robust to trace impurities in the flue gas, translating into significant equipment savings and reduced CAPEX. ■Sectorial Symbiosis: The synthesized Li2CO3 is directly re-integrated into the frit and glaze manufacturing lines at TOR. This replaces virgin, carbon-intensive mined carbonates with a circular alternative, effectively "locking" captured CO2 into a solid mineral form within a commercial ceramic product. By deploying these two routes in parallel, we prove that a single V-CaL capture unit can function as a multimodal industrial refinery, maximizing the value per tonne of CO2 while providing the flexibility required to adapt to varying renewable energy availability and market demands. Methodology: Our methodological framework of CO2HESION is designed to prove that the economic barriers to CO2 utilisation can be dismantled through three principles: 1) Radical Process Intensification; 2) Multi-unit TRL 6 Integration; and 3) Industrial Value-Chain Validation. A central research priority is to characterise the synergistic performance of the conversion CSF reactor and HydroLiC process when directly coupled with the V-CaL capture hub. To move the State-of-the-Art forward, we adopt a rigorous 4 step Data-Driven Engineering cycle: 1) High-Fidelity Empirical Mapping (TRL 4-5): We will execute a series of bench-scale campaigns to map the operational envelope of the CSF reactor, the V-CaL upgrade add-ons for sulphur resilience and the HydroLiC process, specifically focusing on process stability under simulated industrial flue gas. 2) Multivariable Process Modelling: Data from these campaigns will be synthesized by UNIZAR, TRSA and ACC into predictive models that define the optimal operating window for the production of ethylene/propylene mixtures and lithium carbonate. 3) Digital Twin Synchronisation (WP6): These models will be integrated into a DT developed by IRIS, implemented as a multi-agent system (MAS) in which each core subsystem (CO2 capture, thermocatalytic reactor, sorbent loop, hydrogen supply and energy inputs) is represented as an interacting agent. This enables system-level analysis of process behaviour under dynamic conditions, including variability in hydrogen supply, CO2 purity and energy availability. The DT will support what-if scenario simulations and analysis, operational optimisation and stability assessment, ensuring robust performance under industrial conditions, e.g. such as sudden drops in renewable hydrogen from the AEM electrolyser, ensuring the system remains stable during industrial fluctuations. 4) Integrated Pilot Validation (TRL 6): Finally, we will lead the transition to a physical industrial environment, where the digital models are validated against the actual performance of the integrated process: first, with a V-CaL pilot campaign at the TOR site for pure CO2 obtention from industrial flue gas; second, a pilot conversion of this CO2 into ethylene/propylene and Li2CO3 at TRSA and ACC, respectively; and finally, a fully integrated, continuous CCU pilot from synthetic flue gas to ethylene/propylene at TRSA. HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products Page 12 of 48 Scaling an integrated CCU platform involves inherent technical uncertainties that require a proactive scientific approach: ■ The "Interface Fidelity" Challenge: There is a risk that the chemical interfaces between the capture, electrolysis, and conversion units may behave differently at scale than in isolated lab tests. We mitigate this by involving the Engineering-Procurement-Construction (EPC) experts from TRSA at the earliest design stage to ensure "Scale-up by Design." ■ Dynamic Stability Challenge: The coupling of a steady-state chemical reactor (CSF) with an intermittent energy source (AEM electrolysis) creates a control challenge. To overcome this, our methodology relies on advanced monitoring and DT-based system coordination developed by IRIS, enabling the management of interactions between intermittent hydrogen supply and continuous conversion processes, to maintain 500+ hours of cumulative operations. This integrated methodology ensures that efficiency and cost reduction targets are not just theoretical projections, but are backed by a transparent, auditable trail of empirical evidence and industrial validation. Carbon capture methodology: Our methodology is designed around the representative flue gas stream generated at TOR’s ceramic production site, characterised by high temperatures and variable CO2 concentrations in the range of 15–40 vol.%, together with impurities such as particulate matter and sulphur-containing species (see Industrial Use Cases and Figure 5 below). Due to the presence of acid contaminants, which may react irreversibly with the Ca-based sorbent and lead to progressive deactivation of the active capture material, we will design and install removable modular sulphur trap cassettes within the capture reactor to protect sorbent performance and ensure stable long-term operation. At lab scale, we will carry out dedicated test-rig experiments to develop and optimize the sulphur trap system, including material screening, sulphur uptake and breakthrough testing, and cassette geometry optimisation under representative flue gas conditions. The outcomes of these experiments will define the design basis for subsequent scale-up and integration into the V-CaL pilot plant. We will perform CO2 capture using intensified V-CaL, generating a concentrated CO2 stream through vacuum-assisted sorbent regeneration and enabling efficient heat integration with high-temperature industrial flue gases. The purified CO2 will then be directly coupled to the olefin and Li2CO3 valorisation routes for end-to-end capture-to-product validation under relevant industrial conditions. CO2 to Olefins methodology: Our methodology for the CSF reactor within the CO2HESION project is defined by a sophisticated approach to process intensification that integrates chemical synthesis and in-situ separation into a single, high-efficiency stage. This methodology is centred on the deployment of a dual-solid phase architecture, where a tandem catalyst is specifically engineered to work alongside a high-capacity, regenerable sorbent. The tandem catalyst will be composed of a conventional catalyst for CO2 hydrogenation to methanol (such as Cu/ZnO/Al2O3) and a methanol to olefins catalyst (such as SAPO-34). Both materials must be integrated in each catalytic particle to ensure the best performance. The catalytic particles will integrate both materials in an optimized ratio, with a suitable particle size to be employed in the fluidized bed reactor. 1.2 Methodology Overall methodology: We employ a concurrent engineering methodology in which material science, manufacturing process development, digital intelligence, durability validation, and societal co-creation advance simultaneously through tightly coupled feedback loops. This architecture responds to our central technical challenge: vitrimer formulation, process parameters, sensor integration, and circularity performance are mutually dependent, and optimising anyone in isolation would produce solutions incompatible with the others. We pursue the seven project objectives (O1→O7) in parallel across three research phases that progressively elevate technology readiness from laboratory proof-of-concept (TRL 3 →4) through pilot-line validation (TRL 5) to full-scale demonstrator verification (TRL6), with formal decision gates at each phase boundary. Our methodology is organised into six interconnected workstreams (WS). WS1 addresses vitrimer material design and engineering (O1), delivering novel UP/VE resin formulations and reversible adhesives (R1, R2). WS2 covers smart manufacturing and embedded sensing (O2, O3), producing validated sensor-actuator systems and automated manufacturing processes (R3, R4). WS3 develops durability assessment and functional validation methodologies (O4), generating service-life models and pre-normative test protocols (R5).
