WS1–Vitrimer Material Design and Engineering (WP1 and WP4) WS1 translates the vitrimer chemistry described in Section 1.1 into industrially viable formulations through a structured screening programme that navigates an unprecedented multi-variable formulation space while integrating safety, cost, and processability as concurrent design constraints. Our work delivers two co-equal Results: industrially scalable SSbD vitrimer resin systems for LCM and pultrusion (R1) and a stimuli-responsive reversible adhesive for modular assembly (R2). Key Innovations: Triple-mechanism vitrimer chemistry in UP/VE resins, combining disulfide metathesis and thermoreversible Diels–Alder cycloaddition for in-service self-healing with transesterification for end-of-life reprocessing, enabling a multi-stage dynamic response absent from existing vitrimer systems. Intrinsic fire–circularity synergy through reactive phosphorus flame-retardant monomers that simultaneously catalyse transesterification, uniting fire safety and reprocessability in one molecular design. Systematic formulation navigation through a DoE-based screening architecture that manages the coupled trade-offs between dynamic exchange, flame retardancy, cobalt-free curing, and processability across a formulation space whose coupled interactions cannot be predicted from individual component data. Formulation design architecture. Our vitrimer platform is built on a graded thermal response architecture in which three dynamic covalent mechanisms create a multi-stage capability: disulfide metathesis and retro-Diels–Alder cleavage enable in-service healing at 120–150 °C, and transesterification via latent catalysts provides the primary pathway for end-of-life reprocessing at 150–200 °C. At the actuator target temperature of ~120 °C, both disulfide exchange and partial retro-DA are simultaneously active. This overlap is deliberate as partial DA reversion creates chain mobility that assists crack face interdiffusion during healing but introduces a risk of softening under sustained load. Our DoE programme, therefore, includes characterisation of mechanism interaction at shared temperatures through DMA and creep measurements during controlled thermal excursions. Self-healing via dynamic bond exchange targets matrix-dominated damage modes, specifically transverse cracking, delamination, and fibre–matrix interface debonding, where network rearrangement can restore continuity across crack faces. Two design principles govern our formulation strategy. The first is multi-functionality, where components are selected to serve multiple roles within the network architecture. Three disulfide crosslinker families span distinct exchange kinetics, from fast exchanging aromatic architectures where electron-withdrawing groups lower S–S dissociation energy, through flexible aliphatic chains with moderate exchange rates and hydroxyl groups contributing to transesterification, to rigid aromatic architectures providing structural reinforcement of the dynamic network. Reactive diluents similarly serve multiple functions: HPPA contributes hydroxyl groups for transesterification while reducing viscosity; eugenol reduces viscosity through its polymerisable allyl group while contributing bio-based carbon content; and a newly synthesised furoic acid derived methacrylate extends renewable carbon content while additionally providing hydroxyl groups for transesterification. Diels–Alder crosslinkers exploiting furan–maleimide cycloaddition complement the disulfide pathway with thermoreversible dissociative exchange, and tailored VE oligomers with disulfide moieties in the backbone provide dynamic exchange capability while compensating the styrene-free viscosity penalty and reducing dependence on external crosslinkers. This multi-functionality compresses the formulation space, which is essential in a system constrained by the triple elimination of styrene, cobalt, and halogens. The second principle is intrinsic fire–circularity synergy. Conventional additive mineral fillers at high loadings compromise mechanical properties, exceed LCM viscosity limits, and critically would disrupt dynamic bond exchange in vitrimer networks. Halogenated alternatives are excluded on regulatory grounds

1.2 Methodology Overall methodology, including concepts, models and assumptions Concept: We designed CO2HESION to bridge the gap between high-temperature carbon capture and high-value chemical synthesis, ensuring a TRL increase from 4 to 6 through a structured four-step data-driven engineering cycle HORIZON-CL4-2026-01-MAT-PROD-31 Efficient capture / purification / utilisation of CO2 for the production of competitive products Page 11 of 48 linking: 1) component optimisation; 2) multivariable modelling and interface engineering; 3) system integration and Digital Twin synchronisation; 4) industrial demonstration and end-user certification. Each stage is mapped to a dedicated Technical Work Package (WP 1-WP6), ensuring that our engineering leadership at TRSA is harmonized with the scientific validation provided by UNIZAR and INEGI, while directly addressing the Market Pull from ORL and TOR. The fundamental challenge in conventional CCU is the massive energy penalty associated with the "logistics of CO2", specifically the compression, liquefaction, and transport of purified gas. We disrupt this by positioning the direct integration of V-CaL capture with downstream conversion as a process intensification paradigm. By utilising a Hub-and-Spoke architecture, we eliminate the need for intermediate phase changes, utilising the thermal and chemical state of the captured CO2 directly from the source. This concept is realised via 2 distinct process routes (as illustrated in Figure 1): Route 1: Thermocatalytic Synthesis of Low-Carbon Olefins: Our first route focuses on the production of ethylene (C2H4) and propylene (C3H6) through the integration of the patented CSF reactor with a 25 kW AEM electrolyzer as a green hydrogen source. The thermodynamic novelty here lies in the Equilibrium-Breaking nature of the Continuous Sorbent Feeding system. • Overcoming Thermodynamic Limits: Traditional Reverse Water Gas Shift (RWGS) and subsequent oligomerisation are inhibited by the H2O byproduct, which poisons catalysts and shifts equilibrium backward. The CSF reactor utilises a proprietary sorbent system that continuously removes moisture in-situ, shifting the reaction with an increase of up to 100% in CO2 conversion per pass. • Energy Recovery: By coupling the exothermic nature of the olefin synthesis with the heat requirements of the V-CaL desorber, our system achieves a >40% reduction in total energy intensity. • Industrial Validation: The resulting olefin streams are pressurized and fed to ORL for "drop-in" testing, proving that renewable-powered chemistry can meet the stringent purity standards of the petrochemical sector. Route 2: Mineralisation for Circular Lithium Carbonate: Our second route demonstrates a direct mineralisation pathway that bypasses the need for hydrogen altogether, focusing on the ceramic industry’s "closed-loop" potential. ■Chemical Synergy: Purified CO2 from the V-CaL hub is reacted with lithium-bearing precursors (sourced and pre-processed by ACC from battery recycling) to synthesize Li2CO3. ■Eliminating Purification Extremes: This route eliminates the need for the "ultra-purification" steps required for sensitive hydrogenation catalysts. The mineralisation process is inherently more robust to trace impurities in the flue gas, translating into significant equipment savings and reduced CAPEX. ■Sectorial Symbiosis: The synthesized Li2CO3 is directly re-integrated into the frit and glaze manufacturing lines at TOR. This replaces virgin, carbon-intensive mined carbonates with a circular alternative, effectively "locking" captured CO2 into a solid mineral form within a commercial ceramic product. By deploying these two routes in parallel, we prove that a single V-CaL capture unit can function as a multimodal industrial refinery, maximizing the value per tonne of CO2 while providing the flexibility required to adapt to varying renewable energy availability and market demands. Methodology: Our methodological framework of CO2HESION is designed to prove that the economic barriers to CO2 utilisation can be dismantled through three principles: 1) Radical Process Intensification; 2) Multi-unit TRL 6 Integration; and 3) Industrial Value-Chain Validation. A central research priority is to characterise the synergistic performance of the conversion CSF reactor and HydroLiC process when directly coupled with the V-CaL capture hub. To move the State-of-the-Art forward, we adopt a rigorous 4 step Data-Driven Engineering cycle: 1) High-Fidelity Empirical Mapping (TRL 4-5): We will execute a series of bench-scale campaigns to map the operational envelope of the CSF reactor, the V-CaL upgrade add-ons for sulphur resilience and the HydroLiC process, specifically focusing on process stability under simulated industrial flue gas. 2) Multivariable Process Modelling: Data from these campaigns will be synthesized by UNIZAR, TRSA and ACC into predictive models that define the optimal operating window for the production of ethylene/propylene mixtures and lithium carbonate. 3) Digital Twin Synchronisation (WP6): These models will be integrated into a DT developed by IRIS, implemented as a multi-agent system (MAS) in which each core subsystem (CO2 capture, thermocatalytic reactor, sorbent loop, hydrogen supply and energy inputs) is represented as an interacting agent. This enables system-level analysis of process behaviour under dynamic conditions, including variability in hydrogen supply, CO2 purity and energy availability. The DT will support what-if scenario simulations and analysis, operational optimisation and stability assessment, ensuring robust performance under industrial conditions, e.g. such as sudden drops in renewable hydrogen from the AEM electrolyser, ensuring the system remains stable during industrial fluctuations. 4) Integrated Pilot Validation (TRL 6): Finally, we will lead the transition to a physical industrial environment, where the digital models are validated against the actual performance of the integrated process: first, with a V-CaL pilot campaign at the TOR site for pure CO2 obtention from industrial flue gas; second, a pilot conversion of this CO2 into ethylene/propylene and Li2CO3 at TRSA and ACC, respectively; and finally, a fully integrated, continuous CCU pilot from synthetic flue gas to ethylene/propylene at TRSA. We mitigate this by involving the Engineering-Procurement-Construction (EPC) experts from TRSA at the earliest design stage to ensure "Scale-up by Design." ■ Dynamic Stability Challenge: The coupling of a steady-state chemical reactor (CSF) with an intermittent energy source (AEM electrolysis) creates a control challenge. To overcome this, our methodology relies on advanced monitoring and DT-based system coordination developed by IRIS, enabling the management of interactions between intermittent hydrogen supply and continuous conversion processes, to maintain 500+ hours of cumulative operations. This integrated methodology ensures that efficiency and cost reduction targets are not just theoretical projections, but are backed by a transparent, auditable trail of empirical evidence and industrial validation. At lab scale, we will carry out dedicated test-rig experiments to develop and optimize the sulphur trap system, including material screening, sulphur uptake and breakthrough testing, and cassette geometry optimisation under representative flue gas conditions. 1.2 Methodology  
1.2.1 RePaintIT concept  
We establish an integrated circular architecture, based on intelligent routing and modular processing, that transforms spoiled paints and paint sludges into traceable, high-value secondary raw materials. Our concept combines a digital orchestration layer with modular reprocessing technologies and validated industrial uptake, forming a closed-loop system across paints, construction, ceramics, and polymer value chains. At our core, as reflected in the system workflow below, a digital platform (WP5–WP6, AIMEN/RDC) connects households, retailers, workshops, automotive coating lines, recyclers, and manufacturers through real-time DPP traceability (WP5, ISQ) and batch-level intelligence. Each incoming waste stream is characterised and classified (WP4, PPG), then routed via an AI-driven DST (WP6, AIMEN) to the optimal processing pathway based on technical, environmental, and economic criteria. Four pilot-scale processing lines (WP7–WP9) operate as modular nodes: PEF (KIT), MW/US (FH/NTUA), F-MNPs (PoliMi/Captive/AUTH/NTUA), and SAMS (PoliMi/Tecnom), enabling microbial stabilisation, pigment recovery, and binder liberation under industrially relevant conditions. These lines are sensor-integrated and digitally linked through monitoring systems (WP8, IRIS/Abis/HPI), enabling continuous feedback, optimisation, and dynamic routing. Recovered outputs are classified into three categories (WP9–WP10): (A) direct reuse after stabilisation, (B) reformulated paints, and (C) secondary raw materials for cement, ceramics, and polymer composites. This routing maximises value retention while minimising disposal. By integrating digital traceability, process innovation, and industrial symbiosis, we replace fragmented waste handling with a coordinated, data-driven circular system aligned with EU objectives for climate-neutral industry, resource resilience, and digital product governance. Unlike current approaches (single process or incineration), we combine batch-level traceability, adaptive multi-route processing, and graded upcycling pathways validated by end-users (PPG, Torrecid, SBT), enabling optimisation of technical, environmental, and economic performance. Our methodology follows an integrated system logic across WPs, where data structuring (WP5–WP6), process development (WP7–WP9), and validation (WP10–WP11) are tightly coupled. Digital tools enable routing and decision-making, processing technologies deliver material recovery, and demonstration activities (WP11) validate performance under real conditions. The hierarchy prioritises routes with the highest readiness and industrial relevance. PEF (KIT) and SAMS (PoliMi/Tecnom) are our primary demonstration routes for reprocessing and secondary raw materials recovery, respectively, while MW/US (FH/NTUA) and F-MNPs (PoliMi/Captive) act as complementary routes for streams with higher optimisation potential. Route performance is governed by go/no-go criteria (WP9–WP10) (microbial reduction, recovery yields, energy efficiency, and compliance with end-use specifications). Each batch follows a defined workflow: classification (WP4), routing (WP6), monitoring and QC (WP8–WP9), grading (WP9–WP10), and allocation to validated applications (WP10–WP11). This framework distinguishes high-confidence routes from riskier pathways, ensuring controlled innovation without compromising demonstration outcomes.  
1.2.2 RePaintIT methodology and WP structure  
1.2.2.1 Digital Platform  
The digital backbone of RePaintIT will be a secure, interoperable, and business-oriented EU-wide digital platform and marketplace enabling traceability, valorisation, and structured exchange of spoiled paints and paint sludges. Going beyond conventional waste registries, we will operate as an integrated digital ecosystem connecting households, SMEs, workshops, recyclers, and manufacturers within a trusted environment. Our objective is to convert current liabilities (waste) into tradable assets (secondary raw materials) by combining regulatory compliance, quality intelligence, and market mechanisms. The platform builds on European best practices in waste traceability and circular marketplaces (e.g., CIRCULÉIRE, EU Waste Data System, CIRCULATE). We introduce a novel sector-specific approach tailored to paint waste flows, embedding physicochemical, microbiological, and regulatory parameters directly into digital batch identities. Structured datasets will capture composition, VOC content, biocide concentration, microbial stability, pH, viscosity, origin, and storage conditions, ensuring a level of precision unavailable in generic waste exchanges. A minimum dataset will be defined and enforced across all entries, ensuring consistent batch classification and routing decisions. Each batch will receive a secure QR-coded waste ID linked to tamper-resistant digital records, enabling end-to-end traceability across the value chain. This digital identity framework ties physical materials to verified digital twins, supporting auditability, chain-of-custody transparency, and compliance verification. Interoperability with EU regulations is built into our architecture. The system aligns with the DPP under the Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation (ESPR) and integrates regulatory cross-checks with databases overseen by the European Chemicals Agency (ECHA). Our platform will follow a service supported by scalable computers and storage resources. User roles will be clearly defined, with data input, validation, and access rights assigned to households, operators, recyclers, industrial users, and authorities, ensuring controlled interaction across the system. Role-based access control will govern data visibility and validation responsibilities across actors. Data quality and reliability will be ensured through validation protocols, automated checks, and uncertainty handling mechanisms embedded in the platform. Automated plausibility checks and cross-parameter validation rules will be applied, with uncertainty flags triggering conservative routing decisions. By combining secure traceability, QR-coded digital identities, regulatory intelligence, paint waste management, and a consumer engagement layer, we establish a digitally enabled circular value chain that shifts spoiled paints and sludges from fragmented disposal pathways toward structured, compliant, and economically viable reuse ecosystems across Europe. Our platform will also incorporate secure data management and GDPR-compliant architectures to ensure protection of sensitive industrial and personal data.  
Decision Support Tool  
At the core of our digital system lies the DST that bridges raw data from the platform with actionable guidance for waste managers, logistics, and downstream users. Based on AI-driven resource optimisation tools, the DST will include a logistics optimisation model and a machine learning, multi-criteria optimisation model to classify waste batches and match them to the most appropriate processing route. Parameters such as viscosity, microbial counts, pigment dispersion, VOC levels, and energy balance will feed into models that predict: 1. Direct reuse (e.g., re-blended paints), (Grade A) 2. Purification and recovery (via ultrasonication, PEF, mechanochemistry, etc.), (Grade B) or 3. Diversion into other sectors (cement, ceramics, glazing), (Grade C). Routing decisions will be based on predefined thresholds (e.g., microbial load, viscosity ranges, contaminant levels) and continuously refined using pilot data. The DST will integrate LCA, LCC outcomes, ensuring decisions are technically feasible, climate- and cost-efficient, and aligned with EU Green Deal metrics. Designed for continuous learning, pilot line data will train algorithms in real time to improve classification and prediction. AI will be combined with explainable AI (XAI) for transparent, robust outputs, moving beyond static databases to a self-adaptive digital twin of the paint waste ecosystem, scalable and replicable across EU regions. DST outputs will support decision-making while maintaining human-in-the-loop validation, ensuring that final routing decisions are confirmed by qualified operators where required. In cases of incomplete or uncertain data, conservative fallback routing strategies will be applied to ensure safe and compliant handling of materials. Model validation will be performed against pilot results (e.g., recovery yield, classification accuracy), ensuring robustness of routing decisions. The DST will directly support our project KPIs by optimising recovery rates, CO₂ reduction, and resource efficiency across the value chain.  
Logistics Optimization  
Our logistics optimisation module applies multi-objective optimisation principles commonly used in reverse logistics and hazardous waste network design to ensure that collection and routing of spoiled paints and sludges generate a net circular benefit. The DST evaluates each waste stream based on geographic origin, batch size, paint type, contamination profile, and destination capacity, and computes an optimal routing decision that balances transport distance and cost, associated CO₂ emissions, expected recovery yield, and processing energy demand. Instead of minimising distance alone, our model weighs environmental and financial parameters simultaneously, prioritising routes that maximise material value retention while reducing lifecycle impact. Such approaches are widely applied in closed-loop supply chain optimisation and quality-based reverse logistics. The framework also integrates location–allocation logic to assign waste sources to the most suitable pilot line or downstream industry, considering compatibility constraints (e.g., avoiding mixing chemically incompatible streams), minimum economically viable batch volumes, and time-dependent collection windows. Scenario simulations and sensitivity analyses allow us to test alternative routing configurations under uncertainty (e.g., fluctuating quantities or participation rates), ensuring robust and scalable logistics planning. By embedding this optimisation intelligence within our digital platform, we ensure that transport and processing decisions are justified not only technically but also in terms of lifecycle carbon reduction and economic efficiency.

1.2 Methodology  
1.2.1 Overview of the DBTL Methodology  
We structure the INFUSE methodology around a Design–Build–Test–Learn (DBTL) workflow accelerated by a MIP. We enforce circularity through constraints and SSbD decision gates, and enable it by FAIR data standards at every cycle. Our central methodological choice is to generate dense evidence early through combinatorial PVD libraries, simulations, and literature/dataset mining, before committing to expensive bulk LPBF builds. We maintain rigorously bounded transferability claims between the two domains (PVD & LPBF). The DBTL cycle does not assume convergence: each iteration either narrows our candidate space by confirming a prior or expands the constraint set by refuting one, and both outcomes are equally valuable. High-throughput simulations and experimental evidence act as different fidelities. We treat CALPHAD simulation results as low, PVD experiments as middle, and LPBF as high-fidelity data. For shortlisted compositions, we apply a selective post-deposition annealing step that bridges the as-deposited PVD state toward CALPHAD-predicted equilibrium, providing our multi-fidelity framework with a physically grounded intermediate calibration point. The DBTL loop is structured to accept generalized input requirements (target properties, composition constraints, cost, and sustainability bounds) and is therefore applicable to any advanced alloy family or manufacturing route, not solely the HEA-for-turbine use case demonstrated here. We will run simulation and laboratory campaigns coupled via multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization, reducing the number of experiments needed to achieve the desired properties. The following subsections detail each methodological component. Section 1.3.2 covers our computational alloy design strategy (O1, WP2). Section 1.3.3 describes our FAIR digital infrastructure and Materials Intelligence Platform (O5, WP6). Section 1.3.4 presents our high-throughput combinatorial PVD screening workflow (O2, WP3). Section 1.3.5 addresses our circular LPBF manufacturing and the three powder governance loops (O3, WP4). Section 1.3.6 defines our thin-film-to-bulk transferability quantification approach (O4, WP5). Section 1.3.7 specifies our Safe and Sustainable by Design gates, LCA, and circularity metrics (WP7). Section 1.3.8 details our three TRL 6 demonstrators and their validation protocols (O6, WP8).

1.2.2 Circular Design of HEA: Computational Screening and Alloy Selection (O1, WP2)  
Our computational screening layer serves as the lowest-fidelity data. At the broadest level, we use CALPHAD-based calculations with thermodynamic databases (Thermo-Calc with TCHEA and MOBHEA databases) to predict equilibrium and non-equilibrium phase stability, solidification paths, and Scheil-Gulliver freezing ranges for candidate compositions, enabling rapid rejection of alloys prone to detrimental intermetallic formation or excessive solidification cracking susceptibility. We complement these with high-throughput density functional theory and molecular dynamics simulations contributed by our computational partners, providing ab-initio elastic constants and stacking fault energies that serve as additional low-fidelity data in our multi-fidelity framework. Our multi-fidelity Gaussian process regression models serve as surrogates trained on the combined computational and experimental evidence, providing uncertainty-quantified property predictions (hardness, yield strength, oxidation mass gain) that condition our next experimental acquisition plan. We update the surrogate models after each DBTL cycle with conformance-passed datasets, and we recalculate applicability domain boundaries to prevent extrapolation beyond the validated training envelope. All ingested datasets are tagged with reusability metadata, enabling repurposing for downstream optimisation tasks, secondary feature engineering, or transfer learning in application domains beyond the original DBTL cycle scope. 1.2 Methodology Overall methodology, including concepts, models and assumptions The concept: We will design, develop and validate a reversible long-duration energy storage solution based on PCECs for critical infrastructures operating in non-interconnected or compromised grid contexts. We will operate in dual mode, functioning as a high-efficiency water electrolyser during normal on-grid operation to store renewable electricity as hydrogen, and switching to fuel-cell mode during grid outages to supply electricity and heat in fully off-grid operation. By targeting intermediate operating temperatures (≤600 °C), our system improves durability, enables rapid start-up, and reduces dependence on critical raw materials compared to conventional high-temperature electrochemical systems. The modular PCEC architecture supports scalable power and storage capacity, redundancy strategies, and rapid response, making it suitable for mission-critical sites such as hospitals, data centres and essential utilities. We directly address the limitations of current backup solutions by replacing diesel generators and complementing batteries with a clean, silent, zero-emission hydrogen-based system capable of multi-day and seasonal autonomy. Extended storage options, including liquid organic hydrogen carriers (LOHCs) and metal hydrides, further enhance resilience against prolonged outages and supply disruptions. Our methodological approach is structured as a system-driven design and optimisation framework, which defines the architecture, sizing, and operational logic of the solution. This framework guides the development activities we implement across the technical clusters described below. System-driven design and optimisation framework: Our system design is structured around a multi-stage optimisation and modelling workflow. The first step consists of identifying the operational constraints of the electrochemical conversion system, including power limits, ramp rates, start-up behaviour, thermal operating limits, and efficiency characteristics. In parallel, we define grid constraints, including requirements related to frequency support, voltage regulation, power factor limits, and fault ride-through capability. The second step focuses on the dimensioning of the hybrid storage system, including battery capacity and hydrogen storage volume. These parameters are determined as a function of electrochemical stack power, efficiency, ramping capability, and the required autonomy during islanded operation. The third step involves the development of an Energy Management System (EMS) capable of dynamically allocating power between the grid, batteries, and hydrogen system depending on operating conditions, whereby we consider two operational strategies: ■ Grid-connected operation, prioritising economic optimisation and provision of ancillary services; ■ Islanded operation, prioritising system stability and energy balance during outages. The fourth step consists of validating the system through simulation scenarios using optimisation platforms such as HyPRO. These simulations include normal operation, transition between grid-connected and islanded modes, storage constraints, thermal stress conditions, and extreme outage scenarios. Our system design is formulated as a cost optimisation problem minimising the annualised lifecycle cost while satisfying reliability constraints, power balance requirements, and operational limitations. Based on this design framework, we structure the project implementation into 5 technical clusters, each addressing a specific layer of system development, from electrochemical core technologies to system integration and validation. Methodological approach centred around 5 Technical Clusters: Our methodology is structured around 5 clusters, progressing from electrochemical core development to integrated system validation, while embedding safety, sustainability, and digital intelligence throughout. Our approach is inherently iterative, with continuous feedback between modelling, experimental validation, and system-level integration to ensure coherent progression towards TRL5. We have also used these clusters to frame our workplan (as discussed in Section 3.1). Cluster A – Electrochemical Core Technology (WP1 –WP2): This cluster establishes the electrochemical foundation of our system through the coupled development of materials, cells, and stack-level architecture. At material and cell level, we focus on the design and fabrication of proton-conducting ceramic materials, including electrolyte, air and fuel electrodes, and interfaces, processed through cost-effective and low-emission routes. We give particular attention to minimising area-specific resistance (ASR), reducing interfacial losses, and ensuring stable reversible operation under thermo-electrochemical cycling at 500–600 °C. Cell architectures are optimised to achieve target performance (0.8 A/cm² in electrolysis mode and 0.4 W/cm² in fuel-cell mode), while maintaining compatibility with scalable manufacturing and CRM-free material strategies. At stack level, we define scalable architectures through multiphysics modelling and early experimental validation, integrating electrochemical, thermal, and mechanical constraints. Our stack design is coupled with the development of a thermally coherent hotbox concept, including enclosure behaviour, heat loss control, and thermal-gradient management. We explicitly address transient operation, rapid power delivery, and mode-switching behaviour, ensuring that the stack/hotbox system operates within defined thermal limits (ΔT ≤ 30 °C) and is suitable for stable reversible operation. We define thermal management and instrumentation requirements in parallel, enabling validation-oriented design and providing the measurement basis for subsequent Digital Twin calibration and diagnostics. We also develop protective coatings for high-temperature operation to mitigate degradation mechanisms such as oxidation and interfacial instability, contributing to stack durability and operational lifetime. Cluster B – Safety, Sustainability & System Feasibility (WP3): This cluster embeds safety, environmental, and economic considerations into our system design from the outset, ensuring that technical development is aligned with deployment requirements in critical infrastructures. We establish a safety-by-design framework based on structured hazard identification and risk assessment methodologies (HAZID/HAZOP), defining system safety constraints, mitigation measures, and operational boundaries. This enables early identification of critical risks associated with high-temperature hydrogen systems and supports the development of safe operating procedures and system configurations. In parallel, we conduct techno-economic (TEA) and life-cycle (LCA) assessments following recognised methodologies (e.g. ISO 14040/44 for LCA and discounted cash-flow and levelised cost approaches for TEA), evaluating cost-effectiveness and environmental performance across different system configurations and operational scenarios. These analyses consider material choices, system efficiency, operational strategies, and integration scenarios, providing quantitative benchmarks against existing solutions (diesel generators and battery-only systems). The outcomes of this cluster guide our design decisions across the project, ensuring that the system remains economically viable, environmentally sustainable, and compliant with regulatory and safety requirements, while supporting future scale-up and deployment. Cluster C – Digital Intelligence & Control Architecture (WP4, WP6): This cluster provides the digital and control capability to our system, enabling reliable, flexible, and safe operation under dynamic conditions. We develop a modular Digital Twin (DT) architecture, integrating models of the PCEC stack, hotbox, balance-of-plant, and hybrid storage components. We represent subsystems using reduced-order physics-based models combined with data-driven parameterisation, continuously calibrated using experimental data from cell, stack and system-level testing, enabling real-time estimation of internal states (e.g. degradation indicators, thermal gradients). We use transient multiphysics models to simulate start-up, shutdown, load variations, and electrolysis ↔ fuel-cell switching, providing predictive capability for system behaviour under intermittent renewable input. The DT is structured as a multi-agent system (MAS), where individual agents represent key subsystems (e.g. stack, hydrogen storage, thermal system, grid, load), operating within defined physical and safety constraints and exchanging state information through a coordination layer. This enables distributed simulation of subsystem interactions, analysis of interdependencies, and scenario-based evaluation under normal, transient and fault conditions, including resource-constrained scenarios (e.g. water availability, energy supply or component degradation), enabling constraint-aware decision-making and adaptive system behaviour. Building on this, we design an Energy Management System (EMS) to coordinate power flows between the grid, battery, and hydrogen system. The EMS operates under two regimes: grid-connected mode, focused on optimisation and service provision, and islanded mode, focused on stability and energy balance. We formulate storage sizing, operational constraints, and system behaviour within an optimisation framework that minimises lifecycle cost while ensuring reliability and resilience. We develop DT calibration and diagnostics frameworks using subsystem and early integration data, enabling fault detection, performance tracking, and control refinement. Monitoring and sensing strategies support high-temperature operation and provide the data required for model validation and adaptive control. We implement decision-support functions in a human-in-the-loop framework, combining rule-based logic, optimisation routines and data-driven analytics to support key operational decisions such as mode switching, load prioritisation and system recovery. Our outputs are designed to be explainable and traceable, ensuring operator trust and compliance with critical infrastructure requirements. We integrate safety constraints defined in Cluster B into our control logic, ensuring that system operation remains within safe boundaries under all operating conditions. These constraints are embedded into the Digital Twin as operational limits and alarm conditions, enabling scenario-based stress-testing under high-impact, low-probability events and supporting the definition of safe operating envelopes and fallback strategies. This cluster ultimately delivers a validated, model-based control framework supporting resilient operation in both on-grid and off-grid scenarios. Our outputs provide the control, modelling, and monitoring framework required for subsequent subsystem integration and prototype operation in Cluster D. We conceive the DT in H2SAVE as an operational decision-support layer continuously updated with monitored system data. It will estimate non-directly measurable states, enforce safety-aware operating envelopes, support electrolysis/fuel-cell switching decisions, prioritise critical loads under constrained conditions, and provide explainable recommendations to operators and the EMS during normal, stressed and emergency operation. Cluster D – Subsystem Realisation & Manufacturing & System Integration (WP5, WP6): Building on the validated designs and control frameworks developed in previous clusters, we translate system concepts into physical subsystems and integrate them into a complete prototype. We fabricate reversible PCEC stacks based on optimised materials and architectures, with attention to dimensional tolerances, sealing, and integration of stack components. We apply advanced manufacturing processes to ensure reproducibility and scalability. 1.2 Methodology 1.2.1 FLAME overall methodology Systems scoping and comparative framework development: We will start with a structured systems-scoping phase to capture how microbial stability and system states are defined, including microbial ecology, food systems science, agroecology and One Health research. Methods include (1) synthesis of frameworks from ecology, microbiome science, food systems and resilience theory, (2) comparative analysis of existing constructs, including stability, resistance, recovery, hysteresis and regime shifts, and (3) expert elicitation workshops involving FLAME-Case leaders, data scientists, infrastructure partners and external advisors to align terminology, assumptions and analytical needs. These workshops will follow a structured MAA (O1), involving researchers, farmers, food industry operators, regulators, policy stakeholders and RI representatives. Participants will be identified with support from the Communications experts (O8) and through project partners (FFoQSI, MSA, FSH), existing networks (e.g. RIs, industry associations), and targeted outreach. Workshops will be organised at key project stages (e.g. initial scoping, mid-term validation and final consolidation), with at ≥3 cross-case workshops and additional FLAME case-specific sessions. Microbiome states: The conceptual framework is translated into a quantitative framework to analyse microbiome behaviour, with a focus on the microbiome states “adaptive”, “stressed” and “maladaptive”, and their tipping points. Key elements include (i) characterisation of these states based on microbial community structure, functional outputs and key signalling processes; (ii) classification of perturbations (physical, chemical, biological and management-related) and their effects on system dynamics; and (iii) identification of stability boundaries and tipping points, where small perturbations can trigger transitions between states. This enables detection of early-warning signals of destabilisation and supports prediction of state transitions. Development of microbial indicators and vulnerability descriptors: FLAME will then develop microbial indicators that translate microbiome states into variables. This will be implemented by: (i) identifying candidate indicators from multi-omics datasets (e.g. diversity indices, abundance of key taxa, functional pathway activity, metabolite profiles, recovery rates after perturbation); (ii) quantifying these indicators using harmonised pipelines applied across FLAME datasets; (iii) linking indicators to metadata (e.g. environmental conditions, diet, processing parameters) to assess their sensitivity to different perturbations; and (iv) selecting and validating robust indicators based on reproducibility and predictive value. In parallel, vulnerability descriptors will be defined as combinations of indicators that characterise system susceptibility to perturbations (e.g. low functional redundancy combined with slow recovery rates). These descriptors will be directly integrated as variables into modelling and AI frameworks (WP3, WP8). Integration and validation: The developed indicators and descriptors will be integrated across WPs and tested within the FLAME-Cases. This will be implemented through application of indicators to time-resolved experimental datasets (WP5–WP7); comparison of observed system responses with model predictions (WP8); validation under controlled conditions (e.g. ex vivo gut models, DMC systems) and real-world environments (e.g. agroecosystems, food-processing facilities) in WP9. Performance will be evaluated based on predictive accuracy and ability to detect early-warning signals and system transitions. Results will be iteratively fed back into indicator selection. Experimental design and statistical validation: Experimental validation in FLAME-Cases follows a design to ensure reproducibility and inferential robustness. Experiments are implemented with biological and technical replications, with replication levels determined a priori based on power analyses using literature-derived effect sizes. Controlled perturbation experiments are structured using factorial designs, systematically varying key factors such HORIZON-CL6-2026-02-FARM2FORK-11 - Integrating a holistic perspective in microbiome research for resilient, competitive and sustainable food systems Page 10 of 45 as stressors (e.g. temperature, sanitation regimes, dietary shifts) and community configurations (e.g. DMC compositions). Longitudinal sampling schemes are applied to capture temporal dynamics and recovery trajectories. Statistical analysis is performed using mixed-effects models to account for hierarchical data structures (e.g. repeated measures, batch effects, site variability), complemented by time-series and longitudinal analysis approaches. Three cases have been identified by the consortium as complementary systems representing key domains of the farm-to-fork continuum: agroecosystems, food-processing environments, and the human gut microbiome. In FLAME-Case 1 (Agro-Ecosystem Resilience), the methodological approach is based on longitudinal analysis of microbiomes across representative European farming systems spanning climatic gradients and contrasting production strategies, including regenerative and intensive agriculture. The design focuses on key ecological transmission interfaces along the soil-to-food continuum. Sampling strategies capture interconnected system compartments, including soil, rhizosphere, animals, farmer’s hands and wastewater environments. This systems-level approach allows high resolution of microbial transmission pathways, persistence patterns, and functional connectivity across compartments. Temporal sampling is incorporated to capture variability associated with environmental stressors, such as heat and water limitation. For farmer-hand samples, metadata will capture, e.g. gender, age, farm context, recent activities, contact with animals, soil and equipment, hygiene status, and environmental conditions. For animal samples, metadata will include, e.g. animal species, age, production type, diet and feeding regime, housing conditions, antimicrobial treatments, and contact with environmental compartments (e.g. pasture, indoor housing, manure systems). Harmonised and standardised protocols are applied across all sampling and processing steps to ensure cross-case comparability and compatibility with downstream multi-omics analyses. Metagenomics, complemented by untargeted and targeted metabolomics and strain-resolved analyses, is used to characterise community function. AMR and virulence analysis (bacteria and fungi) are conducted. These datasets are integrated with transmission mapping approaches to reconstruct microbial flows and are incorporated into the FLAME-Map. Aligned with the Intergovernmental Science-Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services (IPBES) Nexus framework, this case links microbiome dynamics to food production, environmental and animal health, water and soil systems, biodiversity, and climate-related stressors. FLAME-Case 2 (Food Processing and Production Resilience) builds on an existing Europe-wide microbiological baseline of food processing environments from the EU project MASTER and focuses on the functional characterisation of microbiome states under industrial conditions. Instead of generating new baseline datasets, the approach leverages existing large-scale microbiome data and existing strain collections from MASTER partners (members of this consortium) to define representative processing-associated microbial communities. DMCs are constructed and subjected to controlled experimental perturbations reflecting key industrial stressors, including variations in raw materials (coming from FLAME-Case 1), sanitation regimes, pathogen and micro- and nanoplastics introduction. These perturbations are applied within controlled experimental systems to quantify resilience properties. Longitudinal multi-omics analyses, including metagenomics and metabolomics will be done. Predictions derived from these experiments are subsequently evaluated in two processing environments that were also sampled in MASTER (Austria and Spain), enabling comparison of microbiome state indicators and assessment of their transferability across industrial contexts. Here, we will also include using a rapid “quantidentification” method from Spore.Bio technologies, which provides quantification and identification at the same time. FLAME-Case 3 (Dietary Modulation and Human Gut Resilience Model) is based on the hypothesis that defined nutritional shifts induce predictable transitions between alternative microbiome states, which can be quantitatively characterized in terms of stability and recovery potential. Here, we will use time-resolved ex vivo gut models that enable precise control over environmental conditions, perturbation intensity, and duration. Complementary systems, including a batch-based distal colon simulation and dynamic gut ecosystem model, will be applied to capture ecological dynamics. Microbial communities derived from human faeces samples will be subjected ex vivo to a controlled dietary perturbation (shift from animal-based to plant-based, fibre rich diet). In parallel, AI-driven and data-integrative approaches will be employed to identify microbial taxa and functional signatures associated with diet responsiveness and host-relevant health outcomes. By leveraging large-scale metagenomic datasets, we will detect key microbial players involved in state transition dynamics, including currently uncultured taxa. These insights will guide targeted microbial isolation strategies and DMC design. We will further leverage novel approaches (a ‘reverse ecology’ approach) that group microbes into those occupying a unique, cohesive ecological niche in vivo to assist with strain selection. Building on these data, we will construct functionally distinct DMCs representing alternative microbiome states, enabling systematic testing of microbiome behaviour under defined perturbations. Preliminary works from partners’ studies already showed that these will include species not yet isolated, so targeted isolation of these taxa will be pursued. Emphasis will be placed on identifying early-warning signals that precede loss of functional stability, such as shifts in community composition, metabolic activity, or interaction networks. Data integration: All data generated across FLAME-Cases and already existing high-quality datasets are made accessible in the FLAME-Hub, including metadata and ontology-aligned descriptors. Data ingestion pipelines ensure consistent formatting and quality control across multi-omics datasets, metadata and experimental outputs. To support harmonised data capture from the earliest stages of the project, FLAME will implement a centralised, FAIR- HORIZON-CL6-2026-02-FARM2FORK-11 - Integrating a holistic perspective in microbiome research for resilient, competitive and sustainable food systems Page 11 of 45 compliant management layer based on systems such as SODAR. This layer will be used to structure samples, assays, protocols and experimental metadata. Data harmonisation is aligned with RIs (e.g. ELIXIR, MIRRI) building on existing frameworks (e.g. MIxS standards, FAIR-compliant data formats and RI-supported ontologies). Where required, FLAME will interface directly with RI services and repositories (e.g. ELIXIR tools, ENA, MIRRI/DSMZ, biobanking of samples) to ensure compatibility and long-term data stewardship. Briefly, raw sequencing data will be quality controlled (QC) (MultiQC, fastP) the QC data will follow two different approaches: assembly-free and assembly-based. The assembly free approach will undergo the alignment of the QC-reads against different taxonomic and functional databases for species and strain level profiling (MetaPhlAn, StrainPhlAn, and HUMAnN). Taxonomic profiling will be performed using MetaPhlan 4 to quantify abundances of known and yet-to-be-characterized species (SGBs), while StrainPhlAn 4 will be employed to achieve strain resolution using consensus sequences from species-specific marker genes. From species-level phylogenies spanning multiple metagenomes, we will calculate phylogenetic distances to define strain transmission and track microbial strains across environments. In the assembly-based approach, QC-reads will be assembled into contigs (SPAdes), and contigs will be binned to resolve metagenome-assembled genomes (MAGs) (bowtie2, MetaBat2, SemiBin2, CheckM2). Both contigs and MAGs will be subjected to taxonomic assignment (Kraken2, GTDB-Tk, PhyloPhlAn) and functional annotation (Prodigal, Miniprot, DIAMOND, BLASTP). Specialised pipelines will further characterise antimicrobial and antifungal resistance (TORMES, ChroQueTas), mobile genetic elements (PlasmidFinder, geNomad, IntegronFinder, MEFinder), and biosynthetic gene cluster potential (antiSMASH). These genomic data are complemented by untargeted and targeted metabolomics. Metabolomics is conducted using high mass accuracy instrumentation such as the Thermo QExactive for discovery, and instruments with fast scanning speeds for quantification e.g. Sciex 6500. While LC-MS, GC-MS and MALDI instruments are available, LC-MS is intended to form the core of the metabolomics work. At least one additional new high-end instrument will be delivered this year (2026) for exclusive metabolomics use at the Vetmeduni. Instrumentation is maintained in the mass spectrometry core facility and operated by the expert metabolomics Kirwan group from Vetmeduni. System suitability tests, internal standards, blank samples, pooled QCs and long-term reference QCs are analysed as routine to ensure technical reproducibility. Emphasis is placed on high quality management to produce robust and reproducible results by design, optimizing processes including experimental planning, and measuring the technical variance. The validation built into this study design should ensure that reported results have an optimal chance of being reproducible when tested in another lab. All metabolomics analyses will be conducted to best practice protocols such as those developed by the international metabolomics quality assurance and control consortium (mQACC). For multi-omics analysis, multivariate integration approaches implemented in R (e.g. mixOmics, machine learning frameworks such as random forest and support vector machines) will be used. To complement multi-omics approaches, novel rapid microbial quantification and identification are achieved through the integration of the Spore.Bio technology. This is a label-free, multiplex system capable of detecting microorganisms in complex samples in under 10 minutes. The method relies on biophotonics, and more specifically, multi-modal spectral imaging combined with AI-driven signal analysis to detect and quantify microbial load in near real time. By capturing high-frequency spectral and scattering signatures, the system enables continuous monitoring of microbial dynamics without cultivation, supporting high-temporal-resolution tracking of perturbation responses. High-throughput cultivation and DMC engineering as experimental validation layer: FLAME implements a high-throughput cultivation and DMC framework. Generative AI model training: Generative AI is a type of AI that allows for the creation of new, realistic data. This is often achieved through embedding, a process that unifies all data seen by the model by transferring it to the same multidimensional feature space. Here, similar data are closer together, while more different data are further apart. This allows for mathematical computation and comparison of interactions between vastly different data modalities. Through this, we can accurately simulate real-world interactions based on observed and inferred patterns. This also significantly aids in the integration of non-structured or not fully harmonized data. In FLAME, we will develop embedding-based generative AI models trained on harmonised, openly accessible datasets and iteratively refined with project-generated data. This ensures biologically grounded, robust and transferable models across food-system contexts. User access will be provided through the FLAME-VMB, an agentic AI interface translating natural language queries into transparent analytical workflows with full provenance tracking. The FLAME-AI models form the predictive modelling core of the project, translating the conceptual framework of different microbiome states, boundaries and community transitions into quantitative forecasts. They are implemented as embedding-based generative models, in which microbial communities, their functional outputs and their environmental context are represented as learned latent space vectors. This representation supports cross-modal integration, trajectory prediction and counterfactual simulation of perturbation responses. The design combines biological grounding through mechanistic constraints and ontology-aligned features, predictive capacity through learned multi-modal representations, and continuous correction through assimilation of new experimental and field data. The central predictive tasks are the early detection of community trajectories drifting toward stressed or maladaptive states, and the forecasting of intervention effects across the soil-plant-feed-animal-water continuum (FLAME-Case 1), industrial food-processing environments (FLAME-Case 2) and host-associated gut ecosystems under dietary modulation (FLAME-Case 3). Hybrid digital twin architecture: The FLAME digital twin is built as a layered system. A mechanistic layer encodes established host-microbe ecology and community-dynamics formulations and provides interpretability and hard biological constraints. A data-driven layer of FLAME-AI models then learns the high-dimensional interactions, residual dynamics and latent system-state structure that the mechanistic layer cannot capture on its own. A state-assimilation layer updates the model state as new measurements arrive, anchoring twin predictions to observed system behaviour rather than allowing them to drift into unconstrained extrapolation. This layered design supports case-specific instantiations of the digital twin (agro-ecosystem, food processing, human gut) while maintaining a shared methodological backbone across the project. Multi-omics integration: Shotgun metagenomics (strain-resolved analyses, MAGs), targeted and untargeted metabolomics, rapid microbial quantification data, and metadata are harmonised into a common representation aligned with the FLAME-Map ontology. FLAME-AI models follow an embedding-based strategy, in which modalities, compartments and FLAME-Cases are projected into learned latent representations for downstream prediction. Depending on the data, this may take the form of (i) a single shared latent space, (ii) modality- or compartment-specific spaces combined through mixture-of-experts architectures, or (iii) of hybrid configurations. The concrete design will be selected based on the properties of the curated and project-generated data. Model development and training strategy: FLAME-AI models are developed in 2 stages, ensuring that usable models exist before experimental and field data collection is complete. In stage one, a corpus is curated from harmonised, openly accessible microbiome resources covering the target compartments of all three FLAME-Cases, and base models are pretrained to learn general community representations and cross-domain features. In stage two, these base models are iteratively fine-tuned on FLAME's own multi-omics time series as they become available from controlled DMC perturbation experiments, ex vivo gut models and real-world case study deployments, and are extended to intervention-conditioned forecasting. This staged approach allows model capabilities to be refined in parallel with experimental progress and progressively specialised toward case-specific state boundaries and transition phenomena. Longitudinal transformation and time-series modelling: Microbiome count data are compositional, zero-inflated and typically irregularly sampled, properties which break naive time-series methods. The longitudinal pipeline therefore applies appropriate compositional transformations and a principled treatment of zero-inflation and missingness, aligns the data onto analysis-ready temporal representations with uncertainty propagated to downstream HORIZON-CL6-2026-02-FARM2FORK-11 - Integrating a holistic perspective in microbiome research for resilient, competitive and sustainable food systems Page 13 of 45 models, and detrends against seasonal, environmental and management covariates where relevant. For temporal modelling, again an embedding-based approach is used: community states are represented as learned vectors whose evolution is captured by sequence architectures such as transformer-LSTM hybrids (e.g. transLSTM) or comparable alternatives. As a contingency, should the embedding-based architecture not deliver the required predictive performance on a given FLAME-Case, FLAME-AI models will fall back to well-established compositional time-series approaches applied per compartment, reducing methodological risk while preserving the overall integration and validation framework. Validation pipeline: Validation of the FLAME-AI models and the digital twin is tiered and is designed to assess predictive performance, generalisability and decision-relevance across the three FLAME-Cases. Blocked cross-validation is applied across sites, seasons and FLAME-Cases instead of random splits, which would leak temporal and spatial structure. Walk-forward validations are then run on longitudinal datasets to simulate real deployment conditions. Models are further evaluated prospectively on withheld field seasons and experimental rounds. A final layer of experimental intervention validation tests twin-predicted responses to defined perturbations against outcomes observed in the FLAME-Cases. Performance is reported using calibration and sharpness metrics alongside point-prediction accuracy and is benchmarked against the microbiome state indicators developed in the project's indicator selection workflows. Failure cases are catalogued and fed back into subsequent training cycles, so that FLAME-AI models are refined iteratively in step with experimental and field validation results. Explainability: FLAME-AI models are developed as explainable components of the analytical infrastructure rather than as opaque predictors. Predictive uncertainty is quantified and reported alongside every model output, and predictions that extrapolate beyond the training distribution are explicitly flagged. Each prediction is logged together with the model version, input data references and preprocessing pipeline used to generate it, ensuring that any analytical result can be traced back to the inputs and model state that produced it. Feature attribution and latent-space inspection methods appropriate to the chosen model architectures allow users to relate predictions to the taxa, functions and environmental drivers that shaped them, supporting interpretation by both expert and non-expert users. Virtual Microbiome Bioinformatician (FLAME-VMB) architecture and methodology: The FLAME-VMB is a conversational AI interface that provides natural language access. Its architecture is designed around three principles: accessibility for non-computational users, explainability of outputs, and full provenance for reproducibility. Agentic AI architecture. FLAME-VMB employs an agentic large language model (LLM) pipeline in which the conversational model acts as an orchestrator, decomposing user queries into structured sub-tasks and routing them to specialised tool modules. The architecture comprises: (1) a NLU layer that parses user intent, distinguishing betIen data discovery queries, simulation requests and FLAME-Map exploration; (2) a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module that retrieves relevant biological context (taxon descriptions, pathway annotations, experimental metadata) from curated domain knowledge, grounding LLM responses in verified scientific content rather than parametric knowledge alone (i.e. not based on the pre-training data of the LLM); (3) a tool-calling layer that translates parsed intents into structured API calls against data queries, FLAME-AI models (latent space lookups, digital twin simulations), and the FLAME-Map (graph traversals); (4) a response synthesis layer that compiles API results into interpretable natural language summaries with quantitative outputs, visualisations and confidence indicators. Query-to-result workflow. A typical interaction proceeds as follows: the user poses a question (e.g., "What would happen to the processing microbiome if I switch from chlorine to peracetic acid sanitation?"). The NLU layer classifies this as a digital twin simulation request. The RAG module retrieves context on the relevant microbiome taxa and sanitation agents. The tool layer invokes the food-processing digital twin with the specified perturbation parameters. The response layer returns a predicted trajectory with confidence intervals, highlighting key taxa affected and early-warning thresholds, all linked to the underlying data and model version via provenance metadata. Explainability of outputs. Every FLAME-VMB response includes: (i) the data sources consulted (with persistent identifiers linking to FLAME-Net records); (ii) the model and version used for any prediction; (iii) caveats flagging where predictions extrapolate beyond observed training data. For non-technical users, the interface provides guided natural-language explanations; for experts, raw model outputs and parameterised query access are available. Integration into the FLAME-Hub. FLAME-VMB will be deployed as an embedded module within the FLAME-Hub via REST API, with the Hub providing user authentication, session management and role-based access control. An onboarding module will guide new users through the VMB's capabilities using scenario-based tutorials. Session-based provenance logging. All VMB interactions are logged with: query text, parsed intent, API calls executed, model versions invoked, data sources accessed, and response content. Logs are stored in a structured, queryable format enabling: (i) reproducibility, any past analysis can be re-executed with identical parameters; (ii) regulatory documentation, a complete audit trail of AI-assisted decisions for compliance reporting; (iii) continuous improvement, aggregated usage patterns inform VMB refinement across development phases. Microbiome-state analytics: System dynamics are characterised through early-warning indicators of instability, including increases in temporal variance as well as shifts in network topology and interaction strength. These indicators enable detection of approaching system transitions before observable collapse. FLAME applies clustering and latent state modelling approaches to time-series data, allowing the classification of microbiome configurations into discrete states and transitional trajectories. Microbiome state boundaries are detected using thresholds at which HORIZON-CL6-2026-02-FARM2FORK-11 - Integrating a holistic perspective in microbiome research for resilient, competitive and sustainable food systems Page 14 of 45 small perturbations result in disproportionate structural or functional shifts, indicating loss of stability or transition into alternative regimes. Indicator development, selection and validation: Candidate state indicators are derived from harmonised multi-omics data and corresponding metadata and selected using quantitative feature selection methods, including LASSO regularisation, random forest importance ranking, and mutual information to identify variables associated with system states and transitions. Robustness is evaluated through sensitivity analyses across perturbation scenarios, sampling depth and data subsets. Indicators are normalised and scaled to enable cross-domain comparability across FLAME-Cases. Performance is benchmarked against established metrics (e.g. diversity indices, pathogen loads, functional outputs) using predictive accuracy and stability criteria. FLAME-Hub.
